3.  Formal Report will be submitted to the relevant exam board (e.g., AQA, Cambridge) if necessary.
4.  Candidate Interview – the student will be given a chance to respond to the allegation.
5.  Outcome and Sanctions – decisions may be made by the school or by the exam board, depending on the severity and nature of the case.

nodes_flat.title: Malpractice: Consequences and Student Responsibilities
nodes_flat.summary: This content details the serious consequences of malpractice, such as warnings, loss of marks, disqualification from qualifications, and reporting to UCAS, noting these final decisions are made by the exam board. It also outlines student responsibilities to avoid malpractice, including following JCQ exam regulations, submitting original work, and adhering to teacher guidance on technology and AI.
nodes_flat.content:
Consequences of malpractice can be serious and may include:

*   A warning.
*   Loss of marks for a component or subject.
*   Disqualification from the subject.
*   Disqualification from all qualifications for that exam series.
*   Being reported to UCAS or other institutions (in severe cases).

These decisions are made by the exam board, not the school, and are final.

To avoid malpractice, students must:

*   Follow all JCQ (Joint Council for Qualifications) exam regulations.
*   Attend exams on time and with the correct equipment.
*   Not bring unauthorised materials into the exam room.
*   Submit their own, original work for coursework and NEAs.
*   Follow teacher guidance regarding the appropriate use of technology and AI. Ask if unsure whether something is permitted.

nodes_flat.title: 9.1.8. Communication
nodes_flat.summary: This section details the communication plan for the policy, specifying that it will be shared with students through assemblies, tutor time, and class time, and made accessible to parents via Phidias.
nodes_flat.content:
This policy will be:

*   Shared with students via assemblies, tutor time, and class time.
*   Available on Phidias for parents.

nodes_flat.title: 9.1.9. Monitoring and Review
nodes_flat.summary: The policy establishes a monitoring and review process. It mandates an annual assessment of the policy's effectiveness.
nodes_flat.content: The effectiveness of this policy will be reviewed annually.

nodes_flat.title: 9.1.10. Reference Documents
nodes_flat.summary: The school adheres to JCQ Guidelines for all examinations, with official regulations regarding malpractice detailed on the provided JCQ website link.
nodes_flat.content: The school follows JCQ Guidelines on all exams. Full details of official regulations can be found at: [www.jcq.org.uk/exams-office/malpractice](http://www.jcq.org.uk/exams-office/malpractice)

nodes_flat.title: 9.2. Internal Appeals Procedure & Assessment Reviews (Including AI Misuse Awareness)
nodes_flat.summary: This section details a four-step internal appeals process for assessment decisions, requiring a written appeal within 5 school days and an outcome within 10, alongside procedures for external Enquiries About Results (EARs) which may incur fees or change marks. It also defines academic integrity terms, outlines specific rules for AI tool usage—permitting for planning but prohibiting for generating assessable work unless authorized—and details consequences for misuse, including referral to an Academic Misconduct Panel.

nodes_flat.title: 9.2.1. Scope
nodes_flat.summary: Outlined here are the specific procedures for students to appeal internal assessment decisions and request Enquiries About Results (EARs). It also details the educational processes for promoting academic integrity, including guidelines for the appropriate use of AI tools.
nodes_flat.content:
This document outlines the procedures for:

*   Appealing internal assessment decisions (e.g., coursework, non-exam assessments).
*   Requesting Enquiries About Results (EARs).
*   Educating students about academic integrity, including appropriate use of AI tools.

nodes_flat.title: 9.2.2. Terminology
nodes_flat.summary: This section defines key terminology related to academic assessments and integrity. It clarifies terms such as Internal Assessment, NEA, Appeal, Plagiarism, Collusion, Academic Misconduct, and the role of an Exams Officer.
nodes_flat.content:
*   **Internal Assessment**: Any assessment carried out by the school (e.g., coursework, non-examined assessments) that contributes to a student’s final grade.
*   **NEA (Non-Exam Assessment)**: Coursework or other assessments completed outside of formal examinations, usually under controlled conditions.
*   **Appeal**: A formal request by a student to review an internal assessment decision they believe to be incorrect or unfair.
*   **Enquiry About Results (EAR)**: A process available after external results are published, allowing students to request a review of marking, moderation, or clerical accuracy.
*   **Plagiarism**: Presenting someone else’s work or ideas as one’s own, without proper acknowledgement.
*   **Collusion**: Unauthorised collaboration with others on work that is meant to be completed individually.
*   **Academic Misconduct**: Any action that gives a student an unfair academic advantage or misrepresents their own work, including misuse of AI.
*   **AI Tools**: Software powered by artificial intelligence that can assist with writing, research, editing, or idea generation (e.g., ChatGPT, Grammarly).
*   **Academic Integrity**: The principle of being honest and responsible in academic work, ensuring all work submitted is genuinely one’s own.
*   **Exams Officer**: The member of staff responsible for managing internal and external assessments, including appeals and communication with exam boards.

nodes_flat.title: 9.2.3. Policy Statement and Principles
nodes_flat.summary: This section details a four-step internal appeals process for students disputing internal assessment marks, requiring a formal written appeal within 5 school days and an outcome within 10 school days from a review panel. This internal decision is final and must conclude before marks are submitted to the exam board, with parent appeals also requiring written submission and retention for 5 school years.
nodes_flat.content:
**Internal Appeals Process (for Internal Assessments and NEAs)**

If a student believes that an internal assessment decision is incorrect or unfair (e.g., a mark for coursework or a non-examined assessment), they may follow this process:

**Step 1: Informal Discussion**
The student should first speak with the subject teacher to understand the rationale behind the mark or decision.

**Step 2: Formal Appeal Request**
If the issue is not resolved informally, the student must submit a formal appeal in writing to the Exams Officer within 5 school days of receiving their mark. The appeal must clearly state the grounds for the appeal (e.g., marking error, incorrect application of assessment criteria).

**Step 3: Review Panel**
A panel of staff not involved in the original assessment will review the student’s work and the marking process. The student will be informed of the outcome within 10 school days.

**Step 4: Appeal Outcome**
The outcome will be communicated in writing. This decision is final regarding internal processes. Appeals must be concluded before final marks are submitted to the exam board. Any appeals made by parents must also be done in writing and will be kept on file for 5 school years or until final results are issued for GCSEs / A levels.

nodes_flat.title: 9.2.4. Enquiries About Results (EARs) – External Assessments
nodes_flat.summary: Students can request an Enquiries About Results (EAR) for external exams by providing written consent and submitting it within 2-3 weeks of results day. The process outlines types of EARs like clerical checks and reviews of marking/moderation, noting potential fees and the risk of marks decreasing.
nodes_flat.content:
After the release of external exam results (e.g., GCSEs, A-levels), students can request a review through the EAR process.

**How to Request an EAR:**

*   Students should speak to the Exams Officer or their subject teacher to discuss whether an EAR is appropriate.
*   Written consent must be provided by the student (or parent if under 18).
*   Requests must be submitted within the deadline set by the exam board (usually within 2–3 weeks after results day).
*   The school may charge a fee unless the review leads to a change in the result.

**Types of EARs include:**

*   Clerical check.
*   Review of marking.
*   Review of moderation (for coursework).

**N.B.** Marks being moderated may also go down if the examination board reassesses the work.

nodes_flat.title: 9.2.5. Academic Integrity & AI Misuse
nodes_flat.summary: Students receive annual guidance at the beginning of the academic year on academic integrity, specifically defining plagiarism, collusion, and misconduct. This guidance also covers the responsible use of technology, including AI tools such as ChatGPT and Grammarly.
nodes_flat.content:
**Student Awareness**

At the beginning of the academic year, all students receive clear guidance on academic integrity, including:

*   What constitutes plagiarism, collusion, and misconduct.
*   The responsible use of technology, including AI tools such as ChatGPT, Grammarly, etc.

nodes_flat.title: 9.2.6. AI Usage Policy
nodes_flat.summary: The AI Usage Policy outlines rules for students, permitting AI tools for planning, brainstorming, and revision (if teacher-approved), but prohibiting their use for generating assessable work unless explicitly authorized, and requiring acknowledgment if used as a source. Misuse leads to referral to an Academic Misconduct Panel or Head of Year, potential disqualification, and notification to exam boards for external assessments.
nodes_flat.content:
Students are informed that AI tools:

*   May be used for planning, brainstorming, and revision — if permitted by the teacher.
*   Must not be used to generate or complete assessable work unless explicitly authorised.
*   Must be acknowledged if used as a source in any part of a submission.

**Consequences of Misuse**

Misuse of AI or plagiarism in coursework or assessments will result in:

*   Referral to the Academic Misconduct Panel or Head of Year.
*   Possible disqualification from the assessment or disciplinary action, depending on severity.
*   In external assessments, exam boards may be notified if malpractice is suspected.

nodes_flat.title: 9.2.7. Communication of Procedures
nodes_flat.summary: This policy details the specific methods used to communicate its contents to different audiences. Students are informed through assemblies, tutor time, or in class, while parents receive the policy via Phidias.
nodes_flat.content:
All students receive this policy via:

*   Assemblies / tutor time / in class.
*   Parents receive this policy via Phidias.

nodes_flat.title: 9.2.8. Monitoring and Review
nodes_flat.summary: The policy's effectiveness will be subject to an annual review process. This ensures regular assessment of its impact and continued relevance.
nodes_flat.content: The effectiveness of this policy will be reviewed annually.

nodes_flat.title: 9.2.9. Reference Documents
nodes_flat.summary: The listed documents provide comprehensive guidelines and policies on assessment procedures, academic integrity (including AI usage), student behavior, and the appeals process, complemented by records of internal assessments and appeals.
nodes_flat.content:
*   JCQ Guidelines on Appeals and EARs
*   Imagine Montessori School Assessment Policy
*   AI Usage and Academic Integrity Guidance
*   Behaviour for Learning Policy
*   Records of internal assessments and appeals (where applicable)

children.title: 1. Our Commitment
children.content:
At Imagine Montessori, we have fundamental values that we are committed to providing as a community. These core values are in line with the Montessori philosophy of grace and courtesy, autonomy and curiosity.

*   We respect different cultures and beliefs
*   We value each individual
*   We treat everyone equally and with fairness
*   We understand right from wrong
*   We help each other
*   We listen to and respect each other
*   We understand the consequence of our actions
*   We respect the roles of the people who help us

We plan assemblies and lessons, in particular:

*   PSE (Personal, Social, Emotional) in circle time and assemblies for Early years and Primary students.
*   PSHE (Personal, Social, Health and Economic) in assemblies for secondary students.

to promote these values and teach children to respect similarities and differences, tackling stereotypes and why some people discriminate. We empower children to speak up against discriminatory actions or words and to value tolerance and respect. The lessons and topics at Imagine carefully take into consideration how to best provide a rich learning experience that will have a positive impact on the students’ moral, spiritual, social and cultural development. Visits link with the class topic and the Montessori philosophy of promoting an understanding of the world we live in.
children.summary: Imagine Montessori outlines its fundamental values, rooted in Montessori philosophy, and details how it promotes these through lessons and assemblies to foster respect, tolerance, and anti-discrimination. The school explicitly commits to equal opportunities for all individuals, prohibiting discrimination based on categories like gender, age, race, and religion in accordance with the Equality Act 2010 (revised June 2015), with breaches reported to the school director and the policy monitored annually.

children.title: 1.1. Equal Opportunities
children.summary: The school outlines its commitment to equal opportunities and anti-discrimination for all individuals, including children, families, and adults, in accordance with the Equality Act 2010 (revised June 2015). It explicitly prohibits discrimination based on categories such as gender, age, race, and religion, integrating policy implementation into every employee's job description and establishing a process for reporting breaches to the school director.

children.title: Statement of Intent
children.content:
**Statement of Intent**

The school is committed to providing equality of opportunity for all persons and works in accordance with the following legislation: Equality Act 2010 (revised June 2015).
children.summary: The school's commitment to providing equality of opportunity for all persons is detailed. It specifies that this commitment operates in accordance with the Equality Act 2010, revised in June 2015.

children.title: Commitment to Equality and Anti-Discrimination
children.content: The school takes great care to treat everyone as a person in their own right, with equal rights and responsibilities to any other individual, whether they are an adult or child. The school is committed to providing equality of opportunity and anti-discriminatory practice for all children and families according to their individual needs. Discrimination on the grounds of gender, age, race, religion/belief, colour, creed, marital status, ethnic or national origin, or political belief, has no place within this school.
children.summary: The school is committed to treating all individuals with equal rights and responsibilities, providing equality of opportunity and anti-discriminatory practice for all children and families. It explicitly prohibits discrimination based on gender, age, race, religion/belief, colour, creed, marital status, ethnic or national origin, or political belief.

children.title: Equality and Openness
children.content:
We believe that the school's activities should be open to all children and families, and to all adults committed to their welfare. A commitment to implementing our equal opportunities policy will form part of each employee's job description. Should anyone believe that this policy is not being upheld, it is their duty to report the matter to the attention of the school director at the earliest opportunity.

**Aims Statement**

"In order to make access to the whole curriculum a reality for all pupils, the school needs to foster a climate in which equality of opportunity is supported by a policy to which the whole school subscribes and in which positive attitudes to gender equality, cultural diversity and special needs of all kinds are actively promoted".
children.summary: This section details the school's commitment to openness and equal opportunities for all children, families, and adults, making policy implementation a part of each employee's job description. It establishes a process for reporting policy breaches to the school director and aims to ensure full curriculum access for all pupils by actively promoting positive attitudes towards gender equality, cultural diversity, and special needs.

children.title: Challenging Discrimination and Societal Impact
children.content:
The school is committed to the achievement of quality and equity for all its pupils and staff. Unfair discrimination, in any shape or form, on the grounds of gender, ethnicity, disability, or age will be challenged and positively countered whenever and however it occurs.

"Countering discrimination should not be seen merely as reluctant, token acquiescence to the law. It is part of the process by which we seek to extend choice and opportunity for everyone, thereby moving towards a more just society". This Policy underpins all other School Policy Statements.
children.summary: The school commits to quality and equity for all, actively challenging and countering unfair discrimination based on gender, ethnicity, disability, or age. This policy, which underpins all other school statements, views countering discrimination as a means to extend choice and opportunity for a more just society, beyond mere legal compliance.

children.title: 1.2. Objectives
children.content:
*   To respond positively to the special needs of young people from ethnic minority groups.
*   To ensure that the needs of pupils with disabilities, learning difficulties or social disadvantages are fully addressed.
*   To eliminate racism, sexism, and discriminatory practices in respect of all disadvantaged groups, and reduce prejudice as much as possible.
*   To promote a positive attitude towards the richness provided by individual diversity and cultural variety.
*   To promote equality of opportunity for all staff regarding their terms and conditions of employment and professional development.
*   To prepare, through the curriculum, all children for life in a multicultural, multilingual, multi faith society in Britain, Europe and the world.

As a school we monitor how we can meet diverse needs and advance equality by securing and maintaining excellent teaching, learning and assessment for all pupils.

*   To 'narrow the gaps' in achievement between different groups and national standards.
*   To ensure pupils are free from bullying in all its manifestations including all types of prejudice-based bullying.
*   To deal with unacceptable behaviour and disruptions to learning and to build a cohesive school community where all our pupils can thrive.

This Policy is monitored annually.
children.summary: This section outlines the school's objectives for promoting equality, diversity, and inclusion for all pupils and staff, specifically addressing the needs of ethnic minorities, disabled students, and those with learning difficulties. It details commitments to eliminate discrimination, reduce prejudice, narrow achievement gaps, prevent bullying, and prepare students for a multicultural society, with the policy monitored annually.

children.title: 2. Dress Code Policy
children.summary: Imagine's dress code policy outlines gender-neutral restrictions for safety and focus, defines five key terms like "Dress Code" and "Religious Headwear," and is guided by five principles including inclusivity and practicality. The policy will be reviewed annually by the leadership team, incorporating feedback from students, parents, and staff, and references four internal documents such as the Code of Conduct.

children.title: 2.1. Scope of the Policy
children.content:
At Imagine we promote cultural diversity and value the differences students, staff and families bring to our community. We do not use uniform in the day-to-day of the school as part of our respect for individual expression. However, it is essential we impose a few justified gender-neutral dress code restrictions in order to keep everyone safe and allow them to focus on their work day.

Considering the international community and the variety of cultures we follow certain dress key principles. These principles help us to preserve our cultural diversity and ensure that every member of our community feels valued and able to express themselves.
children.summary: Imagine's policy on dress code is detailed, noting the absence of a uniform to respect individual expression, but outlining the imposition of a few gender-neutral restrictions for safety and focus. These restrictions are based on key principles that preserve cultural diversity and ensure all community members feel valued.

children.title: 2.2. Terminology
children.content:
*   **Dress Code**: Guidelines established to define acceptable attire for students and staff in the school environment.
*   **Health and Safety**: Measures taken to ensure the physical safety and wellbeing of all members of the school community.
*   **Cultural Diversity**: The inclusion and celebration of varied cultural backgrounds, practices, and expressions within the school.
*   **Gender-Neutral**: A policy or practice that does not differentiate based on gender.
*   **Religious Headwear**: Items worn for religious purposes, such as hijabs, turbans, or kippahs.
children.summary: Key terminology relevant to school policies and environment is defined here. It clarifies concepts such as Dress Code, Health and Safety, Cultural Diversity, Gender-Neutral practices, and Religious Headwear.

children.title: 2.3. Policy Statement and Principles
children.content:
At Imagine, we are committed to fostering an environment that celebrates cultural diversity and promotes respect for individual expression while maintaining safety and a focus on educational priorities. This dress code policy adheres to the following key principles:

1.  **Inclusivity**: We respect the cultural, religious, and personal choices of our community members. Our guidelines ensure everyone feels welcome and valued.
2.  **Safety**: Clothing and accessories must support the safety of students and staff, particularly during physical activities or in environments where hazards may exist.
3.  **Practicality**: Attire should enable ease of movement and comfort to facilitate participation in all school activities.
4.  **Modesty and Respect**: Guidelines aim to maintain a balance between individual expression and standards appropriate for a shared learning environment.
5.  **Clarity**: This policy provides clear, gender-neutral expectations to prevent misunderstandings or confusion.
children.summary: Imagine's dress code policy is built on a commitment to cultural diversity, individual expression, safety, and educational priorities. It is guided by five key principles: inclusivity, safety, practicality, modesty and respect, and clarity, ensuring gender-neutral expectations.

children.title: 2.4. General Guidance
children.summary: General Guidance outlines specific dress code requirements, such as prohibiting dangling jewelry and acrylic nails exceeding two millimeters, while permitting subtle makeup (excluding lipstick) from Year 7. It also details rules for headwear, flat and enclosed footwear, PE day attire, and offers support for parents facing clothing challenges.

children.title: Headwear and Footwear Guidelines
children.content:
**Headwear**

*   Hats will only be worn during playground time to cover from the sun.
*   Religious headwear may be always worn, if it does not obscure the vision of the student and must be removed for all PE lessons for health and safety reasons.
*   Hair can be any length and any style. For PE, long hair needs to be tied back for safety.

**Footwear**

*   Flat shoes must be worn outdoors that are comfortable and are enclosed using laces/buckles/zips or velcro and students are able to take off and put on alone.
*   Indoor shoes: closed-toe shoes to help safe walking, nothing bulky or furry, no flip-flops. Anti-slip socks are also recommended.
children.summary: Headwear guidelines specify when hats are permitted, conditions for religious headwear, and hair tying for PE. Footwear rules detail requirements for outdoor shoes (flat, enclosed, self-manageable) and indoor shoes (closed-toe, no bulk, anti-slip socks recommended).

children.title: General Everyday Style Rules
children.content:
**General Everyday Style**

*   Jewellery must not dangle; no hoop earrings or large necklaces. This is for Health and Safety. On PE days, students must be able to remove their stud earrings or other items as required.
*   Subtle make-up can be worn from Year 7 upwards. For example, this includes mascara, powder, foundation, nail varnish, but no lipstick.
*   Students may not wear acrylic nails longer than two millimetres from the top of the fingertip for Health and Safety reasons.
*   No sunglasses inside the classrooms or buildings.
*   Shorts and skirts must cover the upper thighs (¾ length or longer). Cycling shorts are permitted on PE days but must also cover the upper thighs.
*   Sweatshirts/Jumpers/Cardigans can be worn at any length, providing students have a T-shirt underneath.
*   T-shirts must be waist-length or longer.
*   All underwear must be covered by outerwear (no super baggy, low-hanging jeans, etc.).
*   All clothes must be without holes or rips.
children.summary: The rules outline specific dress code requirements, such as prohibiting dangling jewelry and acrylic nails longer than two millimeters, while permitting subtle makeup (excluding lipstick) from Year 7. Additionally, it mandates that shorts, skirts, and T-shirts meet specific length requirements, and all clothing must be free of rips or holes.

children.title: PE Day Attire
children.content:
**PE Day**

*   Students must wear appropriate sports underwear.
*   Shorts/Leggings/Tracksuit bottoms may be worn on the bottom half.
*   T-shirts and sweatshirts on the top half.
*   Trainers.
children.summary: The content details the required attire for students on PE Day, specifying appropriate sports underwear, bottoms like shorts or tracksuit bottoms, t-shirts or sweatshirts for the top, and trainers.

children.title: School Inclusivity and Support
children.content:
As an inclusive school, we ask that our whole community respects these simple guidelines for dressing.

If parents have issues finding suitable items for their children to wear, please enquire within the school, and we would be happy to help you.
children.summary: The school outlines its inclusive dress code guidelines, expecting respect from the entire community. Parents facing challenges in finding suitable clothing are encouraged to inquire within the school for assistance.

children.title: 2.5. Imagine Staff Dress Code (Reference)
children.content:
**a. Acceptable Clothing**

Examples of acceptable clothing include a combination of:

*   Appropriate length skirts and dresses (i.e., knee-length).
*   Blouses (long or short sleeve).
*   Smart T-shirts.
*   Polo shirts.
*   Trousers.
*   Smart Jeans.
*   Chinos.

**b. Non-Acceptable Clothing**

*   Mini-skirts and hot pants.
*   Lycra cycling shorts shorter than mid-thigh.
*   Leisure shorts unless used for P.E./Games or other associated social activities.
*   Combat/camouflage clothing.
*   Transparent or 'see-through' blouses, dresses, or shirts.
*   Tracksuits/Sportswear except for PE and associated social activity.
*   Clothing with tears, holes, and rips.
*   Low-cut T-shirts or blouses.
*   Vest-type tops (spaghetti/shoestring or other strapped tops).
*   Crop-tops.
*   Badges or emblems which may cause offence.
*   Items of clothing bearing logos, slogans, or graphics which could cause offence or are deemed inappropriate for the setting.
*   Indoor wearing of baseball caps/hats.
*   High-heeled stiletto shoes, beach flip-flops (for indoor use only).
*   Clothes which restrict movement and can easily snag on equipment during manual handling manoeuvres (e.g., loose pockets, sequins, large buttons, etc.).
*   Underwear should not be worn in a revealing manner.
children.summary: This staff dress code details specific examples of acceptable attire, such as knee-length skirts, smart T-shirts, and chinos. It also explicitly lists non-acceptable clothing items, including mini-skirts, ripped clothing, offensive logos, and high-heeled stilettos, especially those that restrict movement or are inappropriate for the setting.

children.title: 2.6. Monitoring and Review
children.content: This policy will be reviewed annually by the school leadership team to ensure it remains relevant and effective. Feedback from students, parents, and staff will be actively sought to inform updates. Adjustments will be made as needed to reflect current practices and best practice.
children.summary: The school leadership team will annually review this policy, seeking feedback from students, parents, and staff. Adjustments will be made to reflect current and best practices.

children.title: 2.7. Reference Documents
children.content:
This policy aligns with and is supported by the following documents:

*   Code of Conduct Document
*   Health, Safety and Wellbeing Document
*   Child Protection and Safeguarding Document
*   Staff Handbook
children.summary: This section identifies the specific internal documents that provide foundational support and alignment for the policy, including the Code of Conduct, Health, Safety and Wellbeing, Child Protection and Safeguarding, and Staff Handbook.

children.title: 3. Attendance Policy
children.summary: The attendance policy details specific rules for student presence, including a registration window of 8:45-9:00 AM, minimum attendance expectations (e.g., 80% for CH, 95% for Secondary), and thresholds for persistent lateness (e.g., 12 times/term for IC/CH). It outlines the use of Transparent Classroom and Phidias for recording, processes for reporting absences, and the roles of staff and parents in monitoring and addressing attendance concerns.

children.title: 3.1. Scope
children.content:
Imagine Montessori School values the importance of attendance and punctuality. Children benefit from regular attendance as they adapt to the routines of the learning environment. Imagine Montessori School recognizes its responsibilities to ensure pupils are in school and on time, therefore having access to learning for the maximum number of days and hours.

Our policy applies to all children registered at this school, and this policy is made available to all parents/carers of pupils.

This policy has been written to adhere to the relevant Children Acts, Education Acts, Regulations, and Guidance from the Department for Education.

From the age of 6, parents have the legal responsibility to ensure their child’s good attendance. The administration team works in conjunction with families to ensure that all pupils develop good attendance habits. Procedures in this policy are followed to ensure this happens.

In Early Years (0 to 11 years-old), the morning work cycle begins at 9:00. Children who are persistently late or absent soon fall behind with their learning. Children who are absent from school frequently develop large gaps in their learning which potentially may impact on their progress and their ability to meet age-related learning expectations, especially from Year 1 and above. With children aged 2-6, we are building on their sense of responsibility, routines, and self-confidence, which is why we stress good attendance.
children.summary: The school's attendance and punctuality policy applies to all registered children, emphasizing its importance for learning and development, and noting parents' legal responsibility from age 6. It specifies the Early Years morning work cycle begins at 9:00 and details how persistent lateness or absence negatively impacts learning, especially from Year 1.

children.title: 3.2. Terminology
children.content:
*   **Attendance**: Refers to the presence of students at school during scheduled learning hours.
*   **Punctuality**: The act of arriving on time for school and scheduled classes.
*   **Authorised Absence**: A student’s absence that has been approved by the school for legitimate reasons, such as illness or medical appointments.
*   **Unauthorised Absence**: An absence not approved by the school, even if supported by the parent or carer.
*   **Persistent Absence**: Repeated absences that significantly affect a student’s attendance rate and overall learning.
*   **Morning Work Cycle**: The core learning period beginning at 9:00 AM for Early Years students.
*   **Transparent Classroom/Phidias**: The school’s digital systems used for recording attendance, lateness, and other student-related data.
children.summary: This section defines key terms related to student attendance, including different types of absence, punctuality, and the "Morning Work Cycle" for Early Years. It also identifies the digital systems, Transparent Classroom and Phidias, used for recording attendance and other student data.

children.title: 3.3. Policy Statement and Principles
children.summary: This section outlines the policy's aims to improve pupil achievement through good attendance and punctuality, applying these principles to all age groups including Early Years. It details staff roles in consistent monitoring and promoting a positive environment, alongside comprehensive methods and procedures for identifying, reporting, and addressing poor attendance.

children.title: Policy Aims and Promoting Good Attendance
children.content:
This attendance policy aims to ensure that all staff and families at our school are fully aware of and clear about the actions necessary to promote good attendance.

Through this Policy, we aim to:

*

Improve pupils’ achievement by ensuring high levels of attendance and punctuality.
*   Create an ethos in which good attendance and punctuality are recognised as the norm and seen to be valued by the school.
*   Raise awareness among parents and children of the importance of uninterrupted attendance and punctuality at every stage of a child’s education.
*
children.summary: This policy section details its overarching aim to inform staff and families about actions promoting good attendance. It specifically outlines objectives such as improving pupil achievement through high attendance and punctuality, fostering a school culture that values these, and raising awareness among parents and children about the importance of uninterrupted attendance.

children.title: Early Years and Partnership
children.content:
Ensure that our policy applies to Nursery and Reception aged children to promote good habits at an early age.
*   Work in partnership with parents so that all pupils realise their potential, unhindered by unnecessary absence.
*   Promote a positive and welcoming atmosphere in which pupils feel safe, secure, and valued, and encourage in pupils a sense of their own responsibility.
*
children.summary: This section outlines the policy's application to Nursery and Reception aged children, focusing on fostering good habits and maximizing potential through partnership with parents. It also details the creation of a positive, welcoming, and safe environment to encourage a sense of responsibility in pupils.

children.title: Monitoring and Staff Roles
children.content:
Establish a pattern of monitoring attendance and ensure consistency in recognising achievement and dealing with difficulties.
*   Recognise the key role of all staff, but especially class teachers, in promoting good attendance. We maintain and promote good attendance and punctuality through:
    *
children.summary: The content describes the establishment of a consistent pattern for monitoring attendance, highlighting the key role of all staff, especially class teachers, in promoting good attendance and punctuality. It also stresses consistency in recognizing achievements and dealing with attendance-related difficulties.

children.title: Methods for Promoting Good Attendance
children.content:
Raising awareness of attendance and punctuality issues among all staff, parents, and children.
    *   Ensuring that parents understand the responsibility placed on them for making sure their child attends regularly and punctually.
    *   Equipping children with the life skills needed to take responsibility for good school attendance and punctuality, appropriate to the child’s age and development.
    *   Maintaining effective means of communication with parents, pupils, and staff on school attendance matters.
    *   Developing and implementing procedures for identifying, reporting, and reviewing cases of poor attendance and persistent lateness.
    *   Supporting pupils who have been experiencing any difficulties at home or at school which are preventing good attendance.
    *   Developing and implementing procedures to follow up non-attendance at school.
children.summary: The information details a comprehensive approach to fostering good school attendance and punctuality, encompassing strategies like raising awareness, assigning responsibility to parents and children, and maintaining effective communication. It also outlines procedures for identifying, reporting, reviewing, and following up on poor attendance, alongside supporting students with attendance-related difficulties.

children.title: 3.4. Definitions
children.summary: This section defines authorised and unauthorised absences, detailing the school's registration window (8:45-9:00 AM) and thresholds for persistent lateness (e.g., 12 times/term for IC/CH, 6 for Primary). It also outlines minimum attendance expectations (80% CH, 90% Primary, 95% Secondary), the process for reporting absences, and the roles of staff and parents in monitoring and addressing attendance concerns.

children.title: Definitions of Absence
children.content:
**a. Authorised Absence**

*   An absence is classified as authorised when a child has been away from school for a legitimate reason and the school has received notification from a parent or carer. For example, if a child has been unwell and the parent telephones the school to explain the absence. The school has two categories of Authorised Absence: “Sick Absence” and “Excused Absence”.
*   Only the school can make an absence authorised. Parents do not have this authority. Consequently, not all absences supported by parents will be classified as authorised.

**b. Unauthorised Absence**

*   An absence is classified as unauthorised when a child is away from school without the permission of the school.
*   Therefore, the absence is unauthorised if a child is away from school without good reason, even with the support of a parent.
children.summary: This section defines authorised and unauthorised absences, specifying that authorised absences require a legitimate reason and school permission, which parents cannot grant. It also notes that authorised absences are categorised as "Sick" or "Excused."

children.title: Procedures and Responsibilities
children.content:
**c. Procedures**

Our school will undertake to follow the following procedures to support good attendance:

*   To maintain appropriate registration processes.
*   To maintain appropriate attendance data.
*   To communicate clearly the attendance procedures and expectations to all staff, parents, and children.
*   To have consistent and systematic daily records which give details of any absence and lateness.
*   To follow up absences and persistent lateness if parents/carers have not communicated with the school.
*   To inform parents/carers what constitutes authorised and unauthorised absence.
*   To work with parents to improve individual pupil attendance and punctuality.
*   All staff should be aware that they must raise any attendance or punctuality concerns to the administration team responsible for monitoring attendance.
children.summary: This section details the school's specific procedures for managing student attendance, including maintaining registration and attendance data, communicating expectations to all stakeholders, and systematically recording and following up on absences and lateness. It also outlines the process for informing parents about authorised and unauthorised absences and for staff to report concerns.

children.title: Roles and Responsibilities
children.summary: This section details the specific responsibilities of all school staff, including Class Teachers, Head Teacher, and Administration Staff, alongside parents, for monitoring, recording, and addressing student attendance and punctuality. It outlines processes such as identifying trends, reporting concerns, recording explanations in Transparent Classroom, and contacting parents for unexplained absences.

children.title: General Responsibilities
children.content:
**d. Responsibilities**

All members of school staff have a responsibility for identifying trends in attendance and punctuality. The following includes a more specific list of the kinds of responsibilities individuals might have.
children.summary: The content establishes that all school staff are responsible for identifying trends in student attendance and punctuality. It also indicates that a subsequent list will detail specific responsibilities for individual staff members regarding these duties.

children.title: Class Teacher Responsibilities
children.content:
**e. Class Teacher**

Class teachers are responsible for:

*   Keeping an overview of class and individual attendance, looking particularly for either poor overall attendance, anomalies in patterns of attendance, and/or unusual explanations for attendance offered by children and their parents/carers.
*   Informing the Head Teacher where there are concerns and acting upon them.
*   Providing background information to support referrals.
*   Monitoring follow-up once actions have been taken to correct attendance concerns.
*   Emphasising with their class the importance of good attendance and promptness.
*   Following up absences with immediate requests for explanation, which should be noted in Transparent Classroom as a side note.
*   Discussing attendance issues at parent pedagogical sessions where necessary.
children.summary: Class teachers are responsible for monitoring student attendance for anomalies or poor patterns, reporting concerns to the Head Teacher, and following up on absences with explanations noted in Transparent Classroom. They also provide background for referrals, monitor corrective actions, and emphasize the importance of good attendance to students and parents.

children.title: Attendance Monitoring and Recording
children.content:
**f. Head Teacher**

The Head Teacher is responsible for:

*   Overall monitoring of school attendance on a termly basis.
*   Assessing trends in authorised and unauthorised absence.
*   Contacting families where concerns are raised about absence, including arranging meetings to discuss attendance issues.
*   Monitoring individual attendance where concerns have been raised.

**g. Administration Staff**

Staff in the School Office are responsible for:

*   Collating and recording registration and attendance information.
*   Taking and recording messages from parents regarding absence.
*   Ensuring the Absence/Late Book is completed.
*   Filling in the Transparent Classroom or Phidias for late arrivals and early dismissals.
*   Contacting parents of absent children where no contact has been made.
children.summary: The content specifies the attendance monitoring and recording duties for the Head Teacher and Administration Staff. It details the Head Teacher's role in termly oversight and addressing absence concerns, and the Administration Staff's tasks in data collation, managing absence communications, and contacting parents for unexplained absences.

children.title: Parent Responsibilities
children.content:
**h. Parents**

Parents/Carers are responsible for:

*   Ensuring that their child attends school regularly and punctually unless prevented from doing so by illness or attendance at a medical appointment.
*   Contacting the school office on the first morning of absence.
*   Informing the school in advance of any medical appointments during school time.
*   Making requests for authorised absence in term time only if necessary, as these are not automatically authorised.
*   Talking to the school as soon as possible about any child’s reluctance to come to school so that problems can be quickly identified and dealt with.
children.summary: Parents are responsible for ensuring their child's regular and punctual attendance, notifying the school of absences or medical appointments, and requesting authorized term-time leave. They must also communicate any reluctance their child has about attending school to facilitate early problem resolution.

children.title: Registration and Lateness
children.content:
**i. Registration**

The garden gate is open from 8:45 AM to 9:00 AM. During this time, all children are expected to come into their classroom.

Each class teacher has the responsibility for keeping an accurate record of attendance. Any pupil who is absent must be recorded at the beginning of the morning session.

All attendance records are documented using our systems, which the Head Teacher checks termly. Lateness and absences will be communicated termly via the student report to families.

**j. Lateness**

Once the doors are closed at 9:00 AM, the only way to get into school is via the main entrance. Any pupil who comes into school this way from 9:00 AM will be marked as late in the system and also in the late book. Records are kept of pupils who are late; this is documented on the digital register for each pupil.

Children who have attended a dentist or doctor’s appointment and subsequently come to school later than 9:00 AM will have the lateness recorded as medical.

Children who are persistently late miss a significant amount of learning, often the most important aspect, as the beginning of the day is where the teacher explains the plan and special events of the day.

Where there have been persistent incidents of lateness (more than 12 a term for IC and CH, and 6 for Primary), parents/carers will receive a letter advising them of the concerns, and the school will provide opportunities for parents/carers to seek support and advice to address these issues.
children.summary: This section details the school's registration window (8:45-9:00 AM) and the process for recording attendance and lateness. It specifies that pupils arriving after 9:00 AM are marked late, with medical appointments noted, and outlines thresholds for persistent lateness (e.g., 12 times per term for IC/CH, 6 for Primary) that lead to parental communication.

children.title: Absence and Contact Procedures
children.content:
**k. Absences**

Parents/carers should contact the school on the first day of their child’s absence. When parents/carers notify us of their child’s absence, it is important that they provide us with details of the reason for their absence.

All absences are recorded as either authorised or unauthorised absences on the system. It is important that we receive accurate information from parents with reasons for the child’s absence. This information is used to determine whether the absence is authorised or unauthorised. The teacher has the responsibility to determine whether absences are authorised or unauthorised.

Where we have not received reasons for a child’s absence, the secretary will call to request these details.

**l. First Day Contact**

Where a child is absent from school and we have not received any verbal or written communication from the parent, then we initiate a first-day contact process. The secretary checks all the registers from 9:00 AM to 9:30 AM on a daily basis to identify those pupils who are absent. There are occasions when we are unaware why the child is absent, and we will contact the parent to check the reasons for the child’s absence.
children.summary: Parents/carers must contact the school on the first day of a child's absence with reasons, which teachers use to determine if the absence is authorised or unauthorised. If no communication is received, the secretary initiates a first-day contact process by checking registers between 9:00 AM and 9:30 AM and contacting parents.

children.title: Addressing Attendance Concerns
children.content:
**m. Addressing Attendance Concerns**

The school expects attendance of at least 80% for CH and 90% for Primary and 95% for Secondary.

It is important for children to establish good attendance habits early on in their primary school career. It is the responsibility of the coordinator to support good attendance and to identify and address attendance concerns promptly. In primary school, we rely upon parents to ensure their child attends school regularly and punctually. Therefore, where there are concerns regarding attendance, parents are always informed of our concerns. Initially, concerns about attendance are raised with parents via letters which are sent home. There will be opportunities for the parent/carer to discuss reasons for absence and for support to be given by the school with the aim to improve attendance.
children.summary: This section details the school's minimum attendance expectations (80% for CH, 90% for Primary, 95% for Secondary) and outlines the process for addressing attendance concerns. It explains that coordinators support good attendance, parents are informed via letters, and opportunities are provided for discussion and school support to improve attendance.

children.title: 3.5. Monitoring and Review
children.content:
**Monitoring Attendance**:

Our secretary has the responsibility for ensuring that all attendance data is accurately recorded on Phidias and Transparent Classroom software. Regular meetings are held with the coordinator to discuss all attendance concerns, and appropriate actions are taken following these meetings, such as letters sent to parents or meetings arranged to discuss attendance concerns with parents.

The implementation and effectiveness of this policy will be reviewed annually by the school leadership team. Key performance indicators, such as attendance rates, punctuality trends, and feedback from parents and staff, will inform the review process. Necessary amendments will be made to ensure alignment with current regulations and the evolving needs of the school community.

The Head Teacher will oversee the monitoring of attendance trends termly and provide a summary report to the school board. Persistent or significant concerns will be addressed promptly with relevant families and staff members.
children.summary: The monitoring of student attendance involves the secretary recording data on Phidias and Transparent Classroom, with the coordinator addressing concerns and the Head Teacher overseeing termly trends. An annual review by the school leadership team, using KPIs like attendance rates and punctuality, ensures policy effectiveness and alignment with regulations.

children.title: 3.6. Reference Documents
children.content:
*   Attendance Policy (This Document)
*   Child Protection Policy
*   Behaviour Policy
children.summary: The 'Reference Documents' section lists critical organizational policies, including the Attendance Policy, Child Protection Policy, and Behaviour Policy, which collectively govern presence, safeguarding, and conduct. These documents outline specific rules, procedures, and expectations for all members of the organization.

children.title: 4. Behaviour Policy
children.summary: The Behaviour Policy outlines a comprehensive, Montessori-inspired approach to student conduct, applying to all school community members and emphasizing positive behavior, natural consequences, and conflict resolution. It details a structured system for managing behavior, including a 3-tier intervention model, a Behaviour Flowchart for incidents (Orange/Red), specific consequences like a day at home, and protocols for physical restraint and special intervention support.

children.title: 4.1. Scope
children.content:
This policy applies to all members of the Imagine Montessori School community, including students, staff, and parents. It outlines expectations for behaviour, strategies for fostering a positive environment, and approaches for addressing behavioural concerns. The policy is designed to support children in their personal, social, and emotional development while fostering a sense of community and collaboration.

It is applicable in all settings within the school, including classrooms, playgrounds, and any school-related activities or events, whether onsite or offsite.
children.summary: This policy defines its applicability to all Imagine Montessori School community members, specifically students, staff, and parents. It outlines expectations, strategies, and approaches for behavior, and applies to all school settings including classrooms, playgrounds, and any onsite or offsite school-related activities.

children.title: 4.2. Terminology
children.content:
*   **Positive Behaviour Approach**: A strategy focusing on reinforcing desirable behaviours and guiding children in making appropriate choices.
*   **Natural Consequences**: Outcomes that naturally follow a child’s behaviour without adult-imposed punishments, allowing them to learn responsibility.
*   **Behaviour Flowchart**: A step-by-step guide for staff to manage behavioural incidents in a consistent and scalable manner.
*   **Peace Table**: A designated space where children can resolve conflicts through dialogue and mutual understanding.
*   **Student Support Team (SST)**: A team comprising the Head Teacher and the school psychologist to provide tailored support for behavioural and emotional development.
*   **Classroom Reflection**: A form used to document behavioural incidents and track patterns for minor, major, or unacceptable behaviours.
children.summary: This section defines six key terms related to student behavior management and support. It outlines strategies like the Positive Behaviour Approach and Natural Consequences, tools such as the Behaviour Flowchart and Classroom Reflection, and support structures like the Peace Table and Student Support Team.

children.title: 4.3. Principles and Statements
children.content:
At Imagine Montessori, we respect each child as an individual and their capacity to integrate socially with others. To build on this aspect of community and collaboration, we have certain expectations of appropriate behaviour to build on the happiness and security of every child.

Expectations of behaviour are clearly stated for the well-being of all, and to encourage the emotional support of all, we have instituted a Positive Behaviour approach. This includes the right for everyone to have a safe, positive, and happy experience at school.

Without exception, bullying is not tolerated at Imagine, and we have a separate, specific anti-bullying policy to safeguard against this behaviour.
children.summary: Imagine Montessori implements a Positive Behaviour approach to ensure a safe, positive, and happy experience for all children, built on respect and social integration. Bullying is explicitly not tolerated and is addressed by a separate, specific anti-bullying policy.

children.title: 4.4. Methods
children.content:
Every staff member at Imagine has a pre-designated role to act as an appropriate role model with the use of positive language and behaviour. Children and parents also contribute to these standards with varying degrees of responsibility.

We expect all staff members to:

*   Be respectful to each other and use a positive behaviour approach.
*   Be a good role model to other adults and children in their care.
*   Be engaged in the activities so that the children can use their time with purpose and to their full potential.
*   Provide emotional support to all children in their care to help them meet the behavioural expectations.
*   Work in conjunction with children and parents to aid in the development of the child’s positive behaviour.
*   Work as a team to respond to all children's needs: academical, emotional, social, developmental, etc.
children.summary: Imagine outlines that all staff have pre-designated roles as positive role models, with children and parents also contributing to these standards. Six specific expectations detail staff responsibilities, including mutual respect, engagement in activities, providing emotional support, and working collaboratively with children and parents to foster positive behavior and address all developmental needs.

children.title: 4.5. Responsibility for Behaviour
children.content:
The Head Teachers have overall responsibility for behaviour at Imagine Montessori, which includes personal, social, and emotional development and any related issues arising within this framework.

The Head Teachers will strive to:

*   Keep up-to-date on any new legislation arising, including research in positive behaviour and instituting a Student Support Team, which will include the Head Teacher and school psychologist.
*   Keep staff up-to-date and offer professional development where required to assist them in the function of their roles and responsibilities within the school.
children.summary: Head Teachers at Imagine Montessori are ultimately responsible for student behavior, including personal, social, and emotional development. Their duties involve staying updated on legislation and research, establishing a Student Support Team with a school psychologist, and providing staff with professional development.

children.title: 4.6. Principles of Behaviour Policy
children.content:
*   Grace and courtesy are our key principles as per Maria Montessori’s philosophy.
*   We respect all cultures and differences arising therein.
*   Every staff member at Imagine Montessori School will serve as a positive role model for behaviour.
*   All new staff will have an induction, which will include familiarizing them with the policy and the expectations.
*   We expect all members of our community to treat others with the respect and care afforded to every individual to make them feel included in our community and school ethos.
*   Staff and parents work in partnership to assure that positive behavioural supports are in place for all children in our care, and parents are informed regularly about their child’s behaviour.
*   Parents are also expected to inform the school of any changes in behaviour or family circumstances.
*   The Behaviour Flowchart guides our actions in deciding jointly how to respond appropriately in the event of instances that require our support.
*   We value individuality and understand different children may have different needs.
children.summary: This section outlines Imagine Montessori School's behaviour policy, grounded in Maria Montessori's principles of grace and courtesy, and emphasizes respect for all cultures and the role of every staff member as a positive role model. It details expectations for all community members, the partnership between staff and parents for behaviour support and communication, and references a Behaviour Flowchart to guide responses to incidents.

children.title: 4.7. Appropriate Behaviour
children.content:
Good behaviour should be acknowledged and respected. Even in a Montessori environment, we can thank others for being kind or demonstrating grace or courtesy in situations. This might be through guides in the class or at a peer level.

“Thank you for walking so nicely…”
“Thank you for coming in so quietly…”
“Thank you for waiting…”

This will vary according to the needs of the individuals involved. This positive comment avoids the idea of extrinsic motivation and focuses on recognition of the individual when necessary to help maintain standards.

Standards of behaviour must be made explicit and described in behavioural terms that are relevant to them:

What does respect look like in the playground?

*   Asking if you can take turns or share the equipment.
*   Respecting the space of others and their games.
*   Being kind and including everyone in games.
children.summary: The text outlines the importance of acknowledging good behavior with specific positive comments to maintain standards without extrinsic motivation. It also details the need for explicit behavioral standards, exemplified by concrete actions for respect in the playground such as asking to share equipment and including everyone.

children.title: 4.8. When Behaviour Falls Short of Expectations
children.content:
When there is a minimum expectation (e.g., common courtesy), staff might gently remind a child with a question: “How do you think X felt when you said that?” “Was that a kind thing to do to your friend? How could we fix it?”

If we need to remind children of the core values: Respect, Caring, be Responsible, Calm attitude, we use the key words to restate expectations.
children.summary: Staff address children whose behavior falls short of minimum expectations, such as common courtesy, by asking questions about the impact of their actions and how to rectify them. When reinforcing core values like Respect, Caring, Responsibility, and a Calm attitude, staff use keywords to restate these expectations.

children.title: 4.9. Dealing with Inappropriate Behaviours
children.summary: The school details its comprehensive approach to inappropriate behaviours, emphasizing prevention, early intervention, and guiding children to take responsibility. It outlines a behaviour flowchart and referral forms for tracking minor (Orange) and major (Red) incidents, with consequences like a day at home after 5 minor, 3 major, or any 3 incident reports, and an Extreme Behavior Protocol for high-risk situations.

children.title: Preventative Approach and Early Intervention
children.content: As a team, we strive to work from a preventative perspective rather than reacting. When we intervene at an early stage, we can provide children with tools and strategies at a moment when they can learn them better. However, there are always times when we cannot stop inappropriate behaviours from happening.
children.summary: It details the team's commitment to a preventative approach and early intervention, explaining that providing tools and strategies at an early stage optimizes children's learning, even though some inappropriate behaviors are unavoidable.

children.title: Dealing with Inappropriate Behaviours
children.content:
In dealing with any inappropriate behaviours, our first task is to ensure that the child can understand why that behaviour was inappropriate and take responsibility.

As a staff, we treat all children with respect, and physical interventions are not used except in extreme cases where the safety of others is at stake.

We ask children to describe what happened, explain the impact of their action (if they are unable to do this), and understand why this is inappropriate. This discussion may happen immediately or after, depending on the child.
children.summary: The primary goal when addressing inappropriate behavior is for the child to understand its inappropriateness and take responsibility. Staff treat children with respect, using physical interventions only in extreme safety cases, and guide children to describe events, explain impact, and understand why their actions were inappropriate.

children.title: Behaviour Flowchart and Incident Reporting
children.content: Our behaviour flowchart (see image below) is used to guide actions and interventions in a scalable order of minor and major incidents. A behaviour referral form is used to track behavioural incidents for minor, major, or unacceptable concerns. This will be designated by the person who witnesses the incident/behaviour. Each time a child fills in an incident form, this will be sent home via email to inform families.
children.summary: This section outlines the use of a behaviour flowchart to guide interventions for minor and major incidents, and a behaviour referral form to track minor, major, or unacceptable concerns. The incident type is designated by the witness, and each completed form is emailed to families.

children.title: Consequences and Family Involvement
children.summary: The section details the school's incident management system, outlining the classification of minor (Orange) and major (Red) incidents, the reporting process, and the involvement of the Student Support Team and families. It specifies consequences like a day at home after 5 minor, 3 major, or any 3 incident reports, and includes an Extreme Behavior Protocol for high-risk situations requiring Head Teacher approval.

children.title: General Consequences and Family Involvement
children.content:
After 5 minor incidents or 3 major incidents occur, the school will contact the family to pick up the child, and they will have to remain home the following day. It is very important to keep parents informed and work together to offer the child help at both school and home. The Student Support Team is involved at this stage, and next steps for the parents will be agreed upon.

|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|
|   |   |   |   |   |   |   |
|   |   |
children.summary: The school will contact a child's family to pick them up and keep them home the following day after 5 minor or 3 major incidents. This process involves the Student Support Team, aims to inform parents and collaborate on support, and concludes with agreed-upon next steps.

A consistent 2-step process is detailed for both, involving applying natural consequences, informing the Student Support Team and families, and specific instructions for incident report handling and filing with coordinators like Sheila or Lindsay.

children.summary: The content details consequences for multiple incident reports, specifically outlining that three incident reports result in a "STEP 4 1 DAY AT HOME" consequence. This process involves the Student Support Team contacting the family, requiring the student to be picked up, and a one-day absence from school.

children.title: Extreme Behavior Protocol
children.content: **Note:** We could avoid the flowchart in case of an extreme behaviour such as physical aggression, or high-risk situation and go straight to STEP 4. Any measure applied should be approved by the Head Teacher. |   |   |   |   |   |   |
children.summary: The Extreme Behavior Protocol allows bypassing a flowchart and proceeding directly to Step 4 during physical aggression or high-risk situations. All measures applied under this protocol require Head Teacher approval.

children.title: Immediate Action and Support
children.content: From a Positive Behaviour Support approach, it is recognised that reasons for inappropriate behaviours might require immediate action, including removing the child from the situation until a resolution can be calmly sought. This should be done especially if a child presents a danger to themselves or others. This should not be seen as a sanction nor treated as one. Children will never be left on their own in these occasions.
children.summary: Immediate action, such as removing a child from a situation, is taken when inappropriate behaviors pose a danger to themselves or others, but this is not considered a sanction. During these instances, children are never left alone.

children.title: 4.10. Natural Consequences
children.content: At Imagine Montessori School, we avoid extrinsic motivation, including sticker charts and point systems. We do acknowledge when children make good choices. Natural consequences that occur in line with the behaviour flowchart follow the choices that the children make, and we work towards making them cognisant of taking responsibility for their actions.
children.summary: Imagine Montessori School avoids extrinsic motivation like sticker charts and point systems, instead using natural consequences guided by a behaviour flowchart. This process aims to help children take responsibility for their actions.

children.title: 4.11. Day to Day Strategies
children.content:
To manage and maintain behaviour daily, we have adopted the following:

*   Positive Language used in day-to-day interactions between staff and children.
*   Modelling appropriate behaviour and responding to it, paying special attention to when it comes from children.
*   Circle and group time: to be able to discuss issues arising, teach and practise strategies, etc.
*   Reinstating relationships: when children are involved in an issue, it isn't enough to just “fix” the problem; we work towards giving opportunities to restore the relationship to a cordial and friendly one again.
*   Behaviour Flow Chart: outlines natural consequences ensuing from behaviours of a minor or major nature.
*   Classroom Reflection: used to track instances of a major incident.
*   Positive Behaviour Supports in place, including our Student Support Team, to help with facilitating communication between home and meetings between psychologist and staff, children, and parents to help with next steps and strategies.

We use a 3-tier system which includes the following:

*   At Tier 1, after reminders from teachers, the child can refocus and adapt behaviour to meet expectations.
*   At Tier 2, the behaviour flow chart is followed, and a classroom reflection might be used to track behaviours. After 3 to 5 incidents, parents are called to discuss how to support their child.
*   At Tier 3, the child has a repetitive major behavioural issue, and an individual support plan is required to be put in place and possibly a one-to-one to support throughout the day.

We always rely on prevention rather than intervention, so staff are adequately supported with strategies to prevent children from moving to Tier 2 and Tier 3.
children.summary: Daily behavior management employs strategies such as positive language, modeling, and relationship restoration, supported by tools like a behavior flow chart and classroom reflection. A 3-tier system outlines responses, progressing from refocusing after reminders (Tier 1) to parent involvement after 3-5 incidents (Tier 2) and individual support plans for repetitive major issues (Tier 3), with a focus on prevention.

children.title: 4.12. Special Intervention Support: Tier 3
children.content:
If a child is on Tier 3, where they require specific support to manage their behaviour, a one-to-one support will usually be employed to support during the work cycle and any other aspects of the day, based on the specific needs of the child.

A meeting will be held with the parents to explain the need, the intention, and the strategies the support will provide. Imagine Montessori School, being a private educational institution, cannot provide this support as an internal resource. Therefore, it is critical that parents are involved from the beginning to understand why they need to provide this support for their child. A weekly summary will be provided to the parents by the one-to-one support and monitored by the SST.

The Student Support Team, consisting of the Head Teacher and the psychologist, will meet regularly with the one-to-one support and the teacher to discuss next steps with the positive behaviour intervention.

The individual support plan for the child will outline the strategies to be implemented for learning growth and development. The weekly 1-1 summary will be matched against this plan to track progress made.
children.summary: Tier 3 intervention outlines a process where parents are responsible for providing one-to-one support for specific behavior management, as the private school cannot offer it internally. This includes an initial parent meeting, weekly summaries, regular Student Support Team (SST) meetings, and progress tracking against an Individual Support Plan.

children.title: 4.13. Removal from the Class
children.content:
Following the behaviour flowchart, if a child needs to be removed from the classroom because they present a danger to themselves or others, OR normal reminders are not working and they need to take a break, this should not be presented as a sanction but a normal flow of consequences resulting from their choices.

A choice is always presented to the child before removal from a class, situation, or location. He/she can choose to modify their behaviour to meet the expectations of the situation or can leave the place to take a break. When they are ready, they may come back into the situation, with a clear idea of what their next steps will be.

If a child has a special intervention plan, they may have this included as a strategy to help them self-regulate their behaviour.
children.summary: This section details the process for removing a child from a classroom, framing it as a consequence of their choices rather than a sanction when they pose a danger or normal reminders are ineffective. Before removal, children are always offered a choice to modify their behavior or take a break, with a clear path for their return and potential inclusion in special intervention plans for self-regulation.

children.title: 4.14. Immediate Action
children.content:
Staff have the right to impose immediate action with children displaying inappropriate behaviour. These are always relevant to the particular child and involve the removal of short-term privileges or natural consequences established in the classroom by the children with the support of the guide. Examples of such actions might include:

*   Working on their own rather than with peers.
*   Loss of privilege (e.g., park-time, social lunch).
*   Restricted to work in a particular area.

A “classroom reflection” might or might not be filled in, depending on the severity of the situations.
children.summary: Staff are authorized to take immediate action against children displaying inappropriate behavior, which involves removing short-term privileges or applying natural consequences. These actions, such as working alone or losing park-time, are tailored to the child, and a "classroom reflection" may be used depending on the situation's severity.

children.title: 4.15. Disagreements Between Pupils
children.content:
Part of educating our children involves them learning social skills, including how to manage and resolve conflicts. We refrain from intervening as adults while staying close and monitoring how the children manage the situation and resolve their conflicts. As adults, we can offer them mediation and support towards resolving their problems (strategies).

To do this, we have daily circle time when issues can be brought up, and children are asked to comment on actions or behaviours they noticed in their peers. They are also asked to reflect on their own actions and behaviours. Examples could include:

*   Positive: “Today I felt good when I helped X with their work.”
*   Challenge: “I am working on helping to tidy up after an activity.”
*   Improve on: “I am working on being a kind friend in the park by sharing the materials.”
*   Enjoyed: “I really liked it today when X came to help me put my shoes on.”

They also have the option to resolve conflicts at the ‘Peace Table’. If a child feels they need to resolve something, they might ‘invite’ a friend to the peace table. The child who requested the visit explains why they invited their friend. The other child listens and then has a turn to speak. They stay at the Peace Table until the conflict is resolved. An option is to have a ‘turn to talk’ object they pass to each other to help them take turns speaking and listening. The idea generated is how to fix their problem rather than throw accusations at each other. It is a good idea for an adult to be close by to help mediate if necessary.
children.summary: Children learn conflict resolution through adult monitoring and mediation, utilizing daily circle time for reflecting on actions and a "Peace Table" process. At the Peace Table, children resolve conflicts by taking turns speaking, often with a "turn to talk" object, to generate solutions rather than accusations.

children.title: 4.16. General Principles
children.summary: General Principles outlines strategies for staff to model positive behavior, facilitate conflict resolution using tools like peace tables and circle time, and teach emotional regulation through methods such as deep breathing. It also details prohibited actions, including never using physical force, sending children to corridors, or humiliating them, while emphasizing positive reinforcement and understanding natural consequences.

children.title: General Principles and Positive Strategies
children.content:
The staff are role models when it comes to positive behaviour, demeanour, language, and actions. The following principles apply to all staff:

*   Every staff member at Imagine uses positive strategies to handle inappropriate behaviour and to help children find solutions that are age-appropriate.
*   The peace table is a dedicated forum for this, as well as circle time in a group setting. Discussions should always model positive language that can be used: “Today I felt sad when X didn’t share the playground toys with me,” versus an accusatory, “You didn’t let me play!” We should always support children to gain control of their emotions by taking a break if necessary before they try to resolve their problem (e.g., deep breathing exercises, walk-away).
*   The staff should be aware of triggers for children on Special Support Plans and always work towards prevention rather than intervention.
children.summary: This content outlines principles for staff to model positive behavior and use specific strategies like the peace table and circle time to help children resolve conflicts with positive language. It also details methods for emotional regulation, such as deep breathing, and stresses proactive prevention for children with Special Support Plans.

children.title: WE DO: Positive Reinforcement and Support
children.content:
**WE DO:**

*   Reinforce positive behaviour by acknowledging it as part of grace and courtesy, and we teach children to do the same.
*   Check and reflect on our attitude and language to ensure we are positive role models.
*   Support each other and each child in developing confidence and feelings of competence.
*   Avoid creating situations where children receive adult attention only in response to inappropriate behaviours.
*   Help children to develop a sense of empathy and to recognise the consequences of their choices or actions.
children.summary: Adults actively reinforce positive behavior, model appropriate conduct, and support children's confidence and empathy. They also avoid giving attention solely for inappropriate actions and help children understand the consequences of their choices.

children.title: WE DON'T: Prohibited Actions and Language
children.content:
**WE DON'T:**

*   Never send children out to stand in a corridor.
*   Make them feel sanctions are being imposed as a negative consequence as opposed to a natural consequence.
*   Never use physical force with children or touch them in a rough manner.
*   Never compare a child.
*   Never use techniques to single out and humiliate a child.
*   Do not shout or raise voice in a threatening way in response to inappropriate behaviour.
