children.summary: The content lists prohibited actions, including never sending children to corridors, using physical force, or comparing/humiliating them. It also forbids shouting threateningly or making sanctions feel like negative rather than natural consequences.

children.title: 4.17. Physical Restraint
children.content:
*   In special circumstances, we may use physical restraint such as holding or picking up a child. We ONLY ever do this to prevent physical injury to themselves, other children/adults, or damage to property. When it is practically possible, we let a child know what we are going to do beforehand.
*   Details of a serious incident of this nature are required to be catalogued. We use the “classroom reflection” tracker for this and describe in detail what happened in the ‘note’ section of the form. Details include what happened, who was there, what action was taken and by whom. Details of the incident are kept in the child's personal file. The parent is informed on the same day as per the requirements of the referral tracking form.
children.summary: This section defines physical restraint as a measure exclusively used to prevent physical injury or property damage, with prior notification to the child when possible. It also outlines the process for cataloguing serious incidents on a "classroom reflection" tracker, detailing the event, participants, actions taken, and requiring same-day parental notification.

children.title: 4.18. Inappropriate Language
children.content: Imagine Montessori School is inclusive, and any behaviour that can be categorised as abusive, including negative stereotypes or discrimination of any type, is not appropriate. We immediately make clear the unacceptability of any of these behaviours or attitudes. We do this by explaining what is being done, the harm it causes, and why it is not appropriate. We do not personally blame nor create more stereotypes by labelling individuals as bullies or racists.
children.summary: Imagine Montessori School prohibits abusive behavior, negative stereotypes, and discrimination, addressing such incidents by explaining the harm caused and unacceptability. The school specifically avoids personal blame or labeling individuals as bullies or racists to prevent creating new stereotypes.

children.title: 4.19. Rough Play That Involves Aggression
children.content:
Children can often engage in play that can be based on aggressive themes: superhero and weapon play. Children who engage in this type of play are not necessarily trying to be hurtful. If this play becomes inconsiderate, we need to address it with the following strategies:

*   We understand that this type of play is normal within boundaries and that children like to engage in imaginative play of ‘goodies’ and ‘baddies’. Unless this play becomes hurtful, we do not interfere.
*   We agree ground rules with these children about fantasy play and hurting someone.
*   Encourage children to say no: “Stop, I don’t like it.”
*   We encourage children to learn to accept when someone says “no” and stop.
*   We observe and understand this play and can offer more appropriate alternatives when we see it getting excessively rough.
children.summary: This section outlines strategies for managing children's rough play with aggressive themes, such as superhero or weapon play. It emphasizes allowing such play within boundaries unless it becomes hurtful, and details rules like agreeing on ground rules, encouraging children to say "no," and accepting "no" from others.

children.title: 4.20. Younger Children and Hurtful Behaviour
children.content:
Sometimes young children say hurtful things impulsively without considering what they are saying or the consequences. We use these as teachable moments and help them to manage their feelings in these moments or later, once they are calm. We help young children to learn to empathise through a variety of strategies including role play, modelling behaviour, and circle time. They also learn that they need to fix the problem, instead of just saying sorry.

When these behaviours become problematic, we involve parents to identify the cause and find a solution together. The main reasons for excessive hurtful behaviour usually include:

*   They do not feel securely attached to someone who can interpret and meet their needs: this may be at home or in the school setting.
*   Their parent or carer in the school setting does not have the requisite skills to respond appropriately, and consequently, negative patterns start emerging.
*   The child is exposed to aggressive behaviour at home or from peers at school.
*   In the case of suspected child abuse, we are required to report it to Social Services.
*   The child has a developmental condition that affects how they behave.

In meetings with the school psychologist, we will aim to provide a unified approach in dealing with these behaviours, both at home and in school.
children.summary: The content describes how schools address young children's impulsive hurtful behavior through teachable moments, empathy strategies like role play, and teaching them to fix problems. For problematic behavior, it outlines parental involvement, lists five main causes (including the requirement to report suspected child abuse to Social Services), and details a unified approach with school psychologists.

children.title: 4.21. Monitoring and Review
children.content:
The Behaviour Policy will be monitored and reviewed annually by the Leadership Team and the Student Support Team. Feedback from staff, parents, and students will be incorporated to ensure the policy remains effective and relevant. The review process will consider:

1.  Behavioural incident records and trends.
2.  Feedback from staff and families.
3.  The effectiveness of strategies and interventions.
4.  Alignment with new research or legislation related to behaviour management.

Updates will be communicated to the school community, and training will be provided to ensure consistent implementation.
children.summary: The Behaviour Policy is subject to an annual review by the Leadership and Student Support Teams, incorporating feedback from staff, parents, and students. This process specifically considers behavioural incident trends, intervention effectiveness, and alignment with new research or legislation, with updates communicated and training provided.

children.title: 4.22. Reference Documents
children.content:
This policy should be read in conjunction with the following documents:

1.  Anti-Bullying Policy.
2.  Child Protection and Safeguarding Policy.
3.  Montessori Philosophy Guidelines.
4.  Staff Handbook.
5.  Family Handbook.
children.summary: This section identifies five specific documents, such as the Anti-Bullying Policy and Child Protection and Safeguarding Policy, that provide essential context and supplementary information for a complete understanding of the current policy.

children.title: 5. Anti-Racism Policy
children.summary: The Anti-Racism Policy defines various forms of racism and related terms, outlining Imagine Montessori School's commitment to promoting racial equality and actively tackling discrimination across all school activities and community members. It details responsibilities for staff and leadership, establishes a process for investigating and addressing racist incidents, including disciplinary actions and potential police contact, and mandates annual review and compliance with legislation like the Equality Act 2010.

children.title: 5.1. Scope
children.content: This policy applies to all members of the Imagine Montessori School community, including students, staff, parents, carers, and visitors. It covers all school activities, including lessons, assemblies, extracurricular activities, and off-site trips. The policy addresses both direct and indirect racism, whether it occurs on school premises, during school-related activities, or in any interaction involving the school community.
children.summary: This policy defines its broad applicability, covering all members of the school community, including students, staff, parents, carers, and visitors. It applies to all school activities, from lessons to off-site trips, and addresses both direct and indirect racism regardless of location, as long as it involves the school community.

children.title: 5.2. Terminology
children.content:
*   **Racism**: A form of bullying or discrimination based on an individual’s colour, ethnicity, culture, or geographical origin. This includes both institutional and personal racism.
*   **Personal Racism**: Direct and identifiable acts of harassment or discrimination, such as verbal abuse, physical assault, or racist graffiti.
*   **Equality**: The principle of ensuring equal opportunities and fair treatment for all, regardless of race or ethnicity.
*   **Diversity**: The recognition and celebration of differences among individuals, including cultural and racial differences.
children.summary: The content defines key terms related to race and discrimination, specifically Racism (institutional and personal), Personal Racism, Equality, and Diversity. It clarifies these concepts regarding fair treatment and the recognition of differences.

children.title: 5.3. Policy Statement and Principles
children.content:
Imagine Montessori School is a multi-ethnic community with a significant international student body. It is essential we take steps to educate our community about issues related to racism while highlighting the positive cultural value of a diverse ethnic mix from a global perspective.

Imagine Montessori School will be proactive in implementing its duties and will seek to promote racial equality and good race relations, and to eliminate racial discrimination. Imagine Montessori School is committed to identifying and removing discriminatory practices and any form of racism or racist behaviour.
children.summary: This policy statement outlines Imagine Montessori School's commitment to educating its diverse community on racism and promoting the positive value of ethnic diversity. It details the school's proactive duties to promote racial equality, eliminate discrimination and racist behavior, and remove discriminatory practices.

children.title: 5.4. Aims of the School Anti-Racism Policy
children.content:
The school aims to promote, in the widest sense, a happy and nurturing environment in which individuals are appreciated and respected. All pupils and staff are expected to further this objective. Racism is addressed across the curriculum, for example, in PSHE, Religious Studies, Assemblies, and Circle time.

Imagine Montessori School aims to promote race equality and actively tackle racial discrimination within all areas of school life:

*   To challenge racial discrimination, racist behaviour, racist language, or harassment.
*   Prejudice and stereotyping, however thoughtless or unintentional.
*   To make sure that all students and staff are encouraged and supported to achieve their full potential.
*   To provide an environment which respects and values diversity and shows consideration for the traditions, cultures, and religious practices of people from different racial groups and different geographical regions.
*   To prevent direct and indirect, overt and covert discrimination on grounds of race or geographical origin.
*   To assist in the identification of possible barriers to equality of opportunity for students and staff and to ensure that these barriers are addressed where possible.
children.summary: The school's anti-racism policy outlines its aims to promote race equality and actively tackle racial discrimination by challenging racist behavior, preventing prejudice, and ensuring all students and staff achieve their full potential. It also seeks to value diversity, prevent direct and indirect discrimination, and address barriers to equality, with racism being addressed across the curriculum in subjects like PSHE and Religious Studies.

children.title: 5.5. Definition of Racism
children.summary: This section defines racism as a form of bullying, categorizing it into institutional and personal forms, which can be overt, subtle, intentional, or unintentional. It provides specific definitions for institutional racism, including the Stephen Lawrence Inquiry Report's perspective and its covert nature, alongside examples of direct and identifiable personal racism like physical assault and derogatory name-calling.

children.title: Definition of Racism
children.content:
Racism is a form of bullying. It can be both (a) institutional and (b) personal, overt or subtle, intentional or unintentional.

**a. Institutional Racism**

The Stephen Lawrence Inquiry Report defined this as: ‘The collective failure of an organisation to provide an appropriate and professional service to people because of their colour, culture, or ethnic origin. It can be seen or detected in processes, attitudes, and behaviour which amount to discrimination through unwitting prejudice, ignorance, thoughtlessness, and racist stereotyping which disadvantages minority ethnic people.’
children.summary: Racism is defined as a form of bullying, which can be institutional or personal, overt or subtle, and intentional or unintentional. Institutional racism is specifically defined by the Stephen Lawrence Inquiry Report as an organization's collective failure to provide appropriate service due to color, culture, or ethnic origin, stemming from unwitting prejudice and stereotyping.

children.title: Effects and Identification of Institutional Racism
children.content: When a child is subject to racist bullying or harassment, their behaviour and attainment are likely to be affected. If the behaviour is treated in isolation without taking into consideration the issues and effects of racism, this can be described as institutional racism. The racist element must be explicitly recognised and dealt with.
children.summary: Institutional racism is defined as the failure to acknowledge the racist element when a child's behavior and attainment issues, stemming from racist bullying or harassment, are treated in isolation. The text emphasizes that the racist aspect must be explicitly recognized and dealt with.

children.title: Covert Nature of Institutional Racism
children.content:
This type of racism is also covert and indirect and therefore can be difficult to identify and address. It is often evident in the assumptions, beliefs, and values that affect people’s instinctive responses. It can be demonstrated subconsciously in subtle ways, and Imagine Montessori School recognises the need consciously to challenge such attitudes.

**b. Personal Racism**
children.summary: This content defines institutional racism as a covert and indirect form, evident in subconscious assumptions, beliefs, and values that are difficult to identify and address. It highlights Imagine Montessori School's commitment to consciously challenging these subtle, instinctive attitudes.

children.title: Personal Racism: Definition and Examples
children.content:
This is often direct and identifiable and can be manifested through harassment and offensive behaviour in the following ways:

*   Physical assault against a person or group due to their colour, ethnicity, geographical origin, or culture.
*   Derogatory name-calling, insults, ridiculing, and racist jokes.
*   Racist graffiti.
*   Provocative behaviour such as wearing racist badges or insignia.
*   Verbal abuse and threats.
*   Incitement of others to behave in a racist way.
*   Racist comments within the context of lessons.
children.summary: Personal racism is defined as direct and identifiable, manifesting through harassment and offensive behavior. Specific examples include physical assault, derogatory name-calling, racist graffiti, wearing racist insignia, verbal abuse, incitement, and racist comments in lessons.

children.title: 5.6. Accountability and Responsibility
children.content:
The Senior Leadership Team is responsible for ensuring that the school meets its commitments under its Anti-Racism Policy. In addition, they will provide any necessary help to staff to keep up-to-date with any changes in race relations legislation.

*   The Headteachers will ensure that all staff are aware of their responsibilities and are given the support to exercise this responsibility.
*   All racist incidents that are reported will need to be investigated and recorded.
*   All staff are required to promote racial equality and good race relations.
*   Staff are expected not to discriminate on racial grounds and are expected to attend training or to read information provided by the Headteachers about any change to policy.
*   Staff are expected to always challenge racist and geographically biased attitudes and behaviour.
*   Staff are also expected to be aware of the need to report suspicions of racism to the Headteacher.
*   Where appropriate, the school will be prepared to contact the police if a racial crime has been committed.
children.summary: The section details the responsibilities of the Senior Leadership Team and Headteachers for policy compliance and staff training on race relations legislation. It outlines staff duties to promote racial equality, challenge racist behavior, and report suspicions, alongside the mandatory investigation and recording of all racist incidents and potential police contact for racial crimes.

children.title: 5.7. Action by the School When Racism is Suspected or Reported
children.content:
Alleged incidents of racism should be investigated in line with our Behaviour Policy. We have Incident Report Forms (school drive) which can be used to help systematically record incident details as the first part of an investigation.

Possible disciplinary action by the school:

*   An official warning from the Headteacher.
*   Exclusion from certain areas of school premises and life (such as co-curricular activities).
*   In serious cases or where there is repeated racism, exclusion (temporary) from the school for a fixed term.
*   Continued racist behaviour will be incompatible with staying at the school, and permanent exclusion may be used in such cases.

It is important that the victim of the racist incident is satisfied with the outcome of any disciplinary measures taken. It is also important to remember that anyone who has been a victim of a racist incident has the legal right to report it as a crime to the police.

See the school’s Anti-Bullying Policy for further details about our response to bullying in general.

**Implementing and Reviewing the Anti-Racism Policy**

New staff will be introduced to the policy during their induction.

There will be a formal review of the policy every year, and any necessary amendments will be circulated to staff and families.
children.summary: The school's process for investigating alleged racism involves Incident Report Forms and disciplinary actions ranging from official warnings to permanent exclusion for continued behaviour. It also details the annual policy review, new staff induction, and the victim's right to report incidents to the police.

children.title: 5.8. Monitoring and Review
children.content: The Anti-Racism Policy will be reviewed annually by the Leadership Team to ensure its effectiveness and alignment with current legislation and best practices. Input from staff, students, and parents will be sought to ensure the policy reflects the needs of the community. Any updates or changes will be communicated promptly to all stakeholders.
children.summary: The Anti-Racism Policy will undergo an annual review by the Leadership Team to ensure its effectiveness and alignment with current legislation and best practices. Input from staff, students, and parents will be gathered, and any resulting updates will be promptly communicated to all stakeholders.

children.title: 5.9. Reference Documents
children.content:
*   Behaviour Policy
*   Anti-Bullying Policy
*   Incident Report Forms
*   Relevant legislation, including the Equality Act 2010
children.summary: This section lists foundational policies, such as the Behaviour and Anti-Bullying Policies, which outline expected conduct and protective measures. It also includes Incident Report Forms for procedural documentation and references relevant legislation like the Equality Act 2010, establishing legal compliance standards.

children.title: 6. Anti-Bullying Policy
children.summary: The Anti-Bullying Policy defines bullying comprehensively, including cyber and prejudice-based forms, and outlines its applicability to all school community members and activities. It details specific prevention methods, clear reporting procedures for students, families, and staff, and a structured response system with consequences ranging from warnings to exclusion, all subject to annual review.

children.title: 6.1. Scope
children.content: This policy applies to all members of the Imagine Montessori School community, including students, staff, parents, and visitors. It is relevant to all activities occurring within the school grounds, during school-sponsored events, and in any interactions involving members of the school community, including online or remote communications.
children.summary: This section defines the policy's applicability to all Imagine Montessori School community members, including students, staff, parents, and visitors. It specifies that the policy is relevant to all activities on school grounds, during school-sponsored events, and in any interactions, including online or remote communications.

children.title: 6.2. Terminology
children.content:
*   **Bullying**: Deliberate, repeated, and continuous hurtful behaviour aimed at causing distress to another individual. This includes physical, verbal, psychological, and cyber forms.
*   **Victim**: The individual subjected to bullying behaviour.
*   **Perpetrator**: The individual or group engaging in bullying behaviour.
*   **Cyber-bullying**: Bullying through digital means such as text messages, social media, or emails.
*   **PSHEE**: Personal, Social, Health, and Economic Education.
children.summary: Definitions are provided for five key terms: Bullying (encompassing physical, verbal, psychological, and cyber forms), Victim, Perpetrator, Cyber-bullying, and PSHEE.

children.title: 6.3. Policy Statement and Principles
children.content:
At Imagine Montessori School, we strongly believe that children have a right to study in a safe, supportive, and secure environment. Incidents of bullying threaten this and cause enormous stress to victims. We are therefore strongly committed to the avoidance of bullying in all its forms.

We believe that the principal means of preventing it is through the maintenance of conditions where bullying is less likely to flourish and is more easily detected. At Imagine Montessori School, all pupils will be known to us personally, and it will therefore be easier for staff to detect signs of possible distress. Moreover, we feel that it will be important that pupils have free and informal access to the Head and other staff. A formal anti-bullying policy will be of utmost importance.

As part of our Anti-Bullying Policy, Imagine Montessori School believes that all children and adults have the right to live in a supportive, caring environment in which pupils feel safe and free from bullying and harassment that may include cyber-bullying and prejudice-based bullying related to special educational needs, sexual orientation, sex, race, religion and belief, gender reassignment, or disability, and the use of discriminatory language. Bullying can occur through several types of anti-social behaviour.
children.summary: Imagine Montessori School is committed to preventing bullying by fostering a safe environment where pupils are known personally and have free access to staff, underscoring the importance of a formal anti-bullying policy. This policy defines bullying to include cyber-bullying and prejudice-based bullying related to special educational needs, sexual orientation, sex, race, religion and belief, gender reassignment, disability, and discriminatory language.

children.title: 6.4. Policy Objectives
children.content:
*   To enable all members of the school community to understand what constitutes bullying, making it clear to children, staff, and families that bullying is completely unacceptable and that children and young people have the right to be protected from physical, written, and oral abuse or intimidation.
*   To prevent bullying by raising awareness with all members of the school community, ensuring they have a clear understanding of what bullying behaviour is.
*   To ensure that all members of the school community know and understand the school policy on bullying and follow the established procedures when bullying is suspected or reported.
*   To ensure that all pupils are confident to report bullying if they feel they are being bullied or they witness bullying towards another student.
*   To involve students actively in developing the anti-bullying ethos in the school.
*   To create and maintain a caring, kind, non-violent, and non-threatening atmosphere in the school and raise the school community awareness through educational opportunities that arise, such as PSHE and citizenship activities, circle time, and assemblies.
*   Students are entitled:
    *   To feel welcome and safe and not be made to feel unhappy by others.
    *   Not to be frightened by others.
    *   Not to be touched in an inappropriate manner.
    *   Not to be teased about birth, gender, age, race/ethnicity, sexual orientation, opinion, mental capacity, religion and belief, education, economic status, personality, or any other personal or social circumstance or condition.
    *   Not to be made unhappy by unpleasant comments, notes, letters, text messages, e-mails, phone calls, any form of gossip, or by any other means.
    *   To feel confident to report any concerns.
children.summary: Policy objectives define bullying as unacceptable physical, written, or oral abuse, aiming to ensure all school community members understand and follow established reporting procedures. The section also details specific student entitlements, including protection from inappropriate touching, teasing based on numerous personal characteristics, and various forms of negative communication like text messages or gossip.

children.title: 6.5. Rationale
children.content:
All members of the school have an entitlement to be in an educational environment that is safe, positive, secure, and supportive. Bullying interferes with this.

Bullying can be defined as a deliberate act done to cause distress solely to give a feeling of power, status, or other gratification to the bully. Bullying can range from ostracising, name-calling, teasing, threats, threatening or aggressive language or body posturing, and extortion, through physical intimidation, assault on persons and/or their property. It can be an unresolved single frightening incident that casts a shadow over a child's life, or a series of such incidents.

Staff, parents, and children work together to create a happy, caring, and safe learning environment. Bullying, whether verbal, physical, or indirect, is not tolerated. It is everyone's responsibility to try to prevent occurrences of bullying and to deal with any incidents quickly and effectively.

The victim(s), their friend(s), their parent(s), or other interested people may bring bullying to the attention of any member of staff.
children.summary: This section defines bullying as a deliberate act to cause distress for power or gratification, listing examples such as name-calling, threats, and physical assault. It emphasizes that bullying is not tolerated and can be reported by victims, friends, or parents to any staff member.

children.title: 6.6. Aims
children.content:
*   To provide a safe, caring environment for the whole school community, especially the children in our care.
*   To promote in everyone a positive attitude and high self-esteem.
*   To help individuals to understand and accept the consequences of their own actions.
*   To develop a programme whereby all incidents of bullying can be reported in a secure atmosphere and will be dealt with fairly and appropriately.
*   To help individuals celebrate and value their differences and to acknowledge and respect the differences of others.
*   To instil in all members of the school community that bullying is unacceptable and that reports of bullying will be taken seriously, recorded, and acted upon.
children.summary: The aims detail the school's commitment to fostering a safe, caring environment, promoting positive attitudes, and ensuring individuals understand the consequences of their actions. It outlines a program for securely reporting bullying, guaranteeing that all incidents will be taken seriously, recorded, and acted upon, while also encouraging respect for differences.

children.title: 6.7. Implementation
children.content:
**a. Framework for Preventing Bullying**

Prevention is better than cure, and Imagine Montessori tries to ensure that pupils and adults are educated in anti-bullying strategies. These include:

*   Ensuring that staff always listen to and act on complaints of bullying.
*   Encouraging pupils to tell a trusted adult if they feel threatened in any way.
*   Encouraging the caring and nurturing side of children through explicit teaching of PSHE/Citizenship combined with the Montessori moral awareness which has been developing within the school from when they started in the Infant Community (Pre-Nursery).
*   Working within the school through the curriculum to develop a caring, cooperative, responsible ethos.
*   Providing opportunities to discuss friendships and inter-personal skills, and using drama and role play to meet situations.
*   Ensuring adequate supervision in the playground.
*   Actively discouraging bullying.
children.summary: Imagine Montessori's bullying prevention framework outlines strategies including staff acting on complaints, encouraging pupils to report threats to trusted adults, and fostering caring behavior through PSHE/Citizenship and Montessori moral awareness. The school also develops a cooperative ethos via curriculum, provides opportunities to discuss friendships, ensures adequate playground supervision, and actively discourages bullying.

children.title: 6.8. Definition of Bullying
children.content:
Bullying is behaviour which consists of physical, verbal, or psychological aggression from a student or students towards another student and/or these students place themselves in a position of superiority. This behaviour is repeated, continued over time, and deliberate.

Bullying can take many forms but have these elements in common:

*   Hurtful behaviour which is deliberate.
*   Hurtful behaviour which is repeated.
*   Hurtful behaviour which is continuous in time.
*   Hurtful behaviour which can take the form of physical, verbal, or psychological aggression.
*   Hurtful behaviour from which it is difficult to defend oneself; the bully places himself/herself in a position of superiority.

It can take the form of name-calling, threatened violence, actual violence, intimidation, isolation, ridicule, or other action such as spreading unpleasant stories and gossip, sending hurtful or intimidating text messages, e-mails, comments on social media, and telephone calls.

Communications technology (texting, e-mails, messaging, social network sites, Bluetooth) has also enabled a remote, potentially anonymous channel for bullying, popularly referred to as “Cyber-bullying”.

All bullying is designed to make the victim feel threatened, humiliated, and unsafe. Not all negative interactions between students can be deemed as bullying, and the school works hard to ensure that students understand the difference between bullying and ‘falling out’ or arguing.

All members of the School Community are expected to treat all potential bullying cases with utmost confidentiality, ensuring that all children’s rights are protected.
children.summary: The content defines bullying as repeated, deliberate physical, verbal, or psychological aggression where a student asserts superiority, detailing its common elements and various forms, including cyber-bullying. It also distinguishes bullying from general disagreements and mandates confidentiality for all reported cases.

children.title: 6.9. Preventing Bullying
children.content:
The school is committed to the ongoing development of an anti-bullying ethos throughout the school with students, staff, and parents.

Imagine Montessori School will use the following methods for helping children to prevent bullying. As and when appropriate, these may include:

*   The school rules through circle time, during Personal, Social, Health Economic (PSHEE) and citizenship activities.
*   Social stories and drawings about positive behaviour and role modelling.
*   Reading stories about positive behaviour expectations and bullying, having them read to a class.
*   Making up role-plays.
*   Providing emotional support to give each child the opportunity to express how they feel.

**Methods**:

*   We will watch for early signs of distress in children.
*   We will listen, believe, and act when a child(ren) communicates that they are being bullied.
*   We will intervene to stop the child who is bullying from harming the other child or children.
*   We will explain to the child doing the bullying why his or her behaviour is unacceptable.
*   We will give reassurance to the child or children who have been bullied.
*   We will help the child who has done the bullying to recognise the impact of his or her actions.
*   We support the child who has done the bullying to learn alternate strategies to manage their behaviour.

**Good Practice for Staff**:

*   Be continually aware, watchful, and available.
*   Promote good behaviour and encourage the care of others.
*   Ensure children are appropriately supervised.
*   Report all cases of bullying to the Head.
*   Parents’ involvement and cooperation can be sought, and parents of children involved will be kept informed.
children.summary: Imagine Montessori School's anti-bullying policy outlines specific preventative methods like circle time, social stories, and role-plays, alongside intervention strategies for staff. These strategies include watching for distress, direct intervention, explaining unacceptable behavior to bullies, reassuring victims, and reporting all cases to the Head.

children.title: 6.10. Identifying and Reporting Bullying
children.summary: This section details comprehensive procedures for identifying, reporting, and responding to bullying, outlining specific actions for students (both victims and witnesses), families, and staff. It specifies reporting channels such as Tutors, Key Stage Managers, and the Head Teacher, and emphasizes staff responsibilities for observation, reassurance, and logging incidents.

children.title: Reporting and Responding to Bullying
children.content:
Students and families should report any suspicion of bullying immediately to any of the designated people below:

*   Tutor
*   Key Stage Manager
*   Head teacher

Much of the behaviour which leads to both bullying and intimidation is difficult to observe or can be seen as innocent when it is not. It is therefore essential that staff listen carefully to what children say and watch for any signs that a child may be being intimidated. The staff must always pass on their concern to the other members of staff working with the child and report these concerns to the Head Teacher.
children.summary: Students and families must report suspected bullying to a Tutor, Key Stage Manager, or Head Teacher. Staff are also required to listen for and observe signs of intimidation, share concerns with colleagues, and report them to the Head Teacher.

children.title: Student Actions When Bullied
children.content:
**If you witness any unacceptable behaviour, how you should act:**

1.  **If you feel you are being bullied.**
    Tell someone.
    If you feel you are being bullied, you can tell a member of staff and/or your parent/carer (anyone you feel safe to talk to). It is not weak to do this.
    If you cannot tell anyone at school, you can tell your parents.
    Friends of those who feel bullied should let staff know of bullying incidents.
    No one deserves to be bullied. You have a right for this not to happen to you.
children.summary: This content advises students who are being bullied to report it to a staff member, parent, or carer, emphasizing that it is not weak to do so. It also instructs friends of bullied individuals to inform staff about incidents.

children.title: Student Actions When Witnessing Bullying
children.content:
2.  **If you are a pupil and witness bullying behaviour.**
    Support the child who you observed being bullied by offering your friendship and making it clear that, in your opinion, what is happening to them is unkind.
    Encourage them to speak out on their own behalf by confronting the child who was exhibiting the bullying behaviour, or with their permission, confront that child yourself. Accompany the child who you observed being bullied to a trusted adult or suggest that you speak to an adult on their behalf.
children.summary: This content outlines specific actions for pupils witnessing bullying, such as offering friendship and stating the behavior is unkind. It also details steps like encouraging the bullied child to confront the bully or doing so with permission, and involving a trusted adult directly or by speaking on the victim's behalf.

children.title: Reporting and Responding to Bullying
children.content:
3.  **If you are a member of Staff.**
    Reassure and support the pupils involved.
    Advise them that you are required to pass details on to their Guide and/or to the named person responsible for supporting good behaviour (Key Stage Coordinator/Head Teacher).
    The named person will keep a log of all complaints or incidences of bullying and record the way in which they were dealt with.

4.  **If you are a Parent.**
    Bring the issue to the attention of any member of staff, or to the Head of School, or the named person responsible for supporting good behaviour.
children.summary: It outlines distinct reporting procedures for staff and parents regarding bullying incidents. Staff must reassure pupils and report details to a Guide or Key Stage Coordinator/Head Teacher, who logs the incidents, while parents should report to any staff member, the Head of School, or the designated behaviour support person.

children.title: 6.11. Taking Action
children.summary: This section details the school's structured response to bullying incidents, outlining the process for interviewing both the bullied child and the perpetrator, explaining incident resolution, and documenting their accounts. It further defines a hierarchy of "Actions and Consequences," including formal school warnings, temporary suspensions, and permanent exclusion, alongside procedures for annual policy review and incident reporting.

children.title: Interviewing the child who has been bullied
children.content:
**Interview the child who has been bullied.**

The child who has experienced the bullying will be interviewed by their Guide, who may ask them to write an immediate account of events. The Guide may also invite the child to discuss their own reaction and behaviour towards the child who bullied them. The process for dealing with the incident will be clearly explained to them. The Guide may give them further support and advice if deemed appropriate.
children.summary: A Guide interviews the bullied child, requesting an immediate account of events and a discussion of their reaction to the bully. The process for handling the incident is explained, with further support and advice offered if appropriate.

children.title: Interviewing the child responsible for the behaviour
children.content:
**Interview the child responsible for that behaviour.**

Once the Guide is clear that a bullying incident has occurred, the child who was responsible for the bullying will be interviewed, as will any others related to the incident. Each will be asked to write an immediate account of events. The process for dealing with the incident will be clearly explained to them.
children.summary: This section details the process for interviewing the child responsible for bullying and any other involved parties once an incident is confirmed. They will be required to write an immediate account of events, and the incident resolution process will be clearly explained to them.

children.title: Actions and Consequences: Formal School Warning
children.content:
**Actions and Consequences**

The following sanctions may be applied in accordance with the
children.summary: The content introduces the "Actions and Consequences" associated with a "Formal School Warning." It indicates that sanctions may be applied according to an unspecified set of regulations.

children.title: Actions and Consequences: Suspension
children.summary: This section outlines the formal process for addressing student misbehavior, particularly bullying, detailing the investigation by school staff, communication with families, and the issuance of a formal warning. It defines suspension as a temporary removal from school for policy violations and describes the escalating consequences for continued misbehavior.

children.title: Formal Warning and Investigation
children.content:
school’s Behaviour Policy:

1.  **Formal School Warning - Incident Report - Families Informed.**
    A formal school warning is a written or verbal notice given to a student who has been involved in bullying. It serves as an official acknowledgment of the behaviour and a warning that further actions will be taken if the behaviour continues.

*   **Process**:
        *   **Investigation**: Before issuing a formal warning, the adult who witnesses the incident, the Guide, and the Head Teacher will investigate the bullying incident to gather facts, speak with witnesses, and understand the context.
children.summary: A Formal School Warning is a written or verbal notice for bullying, requiring an Incident Report and informing families. This warning is issued after an investigation by the adult witness, Guide, and Head Teacher to gather facts and understand the incident's context.

children.title: Family Communication
children.content:
*   **Families Informed**: Clear and timely communication with the families of both the bully and the victim, ensuring they are aware of the situation, the actions taken by the school, and the support available. The school contacts the families as soon as possible after the incident has been reported and investigated. This is typically done via phone call, followed by a written notice.
        *   The school may arrange meetings with the families to discuss the incident, share the findings of the investigation, and explain the actions taken by the school. For the family of the bully, this may include discussing the formal warning and any further consequences. For the family of the victim, the focus will be on the support and protection measures in place for their child.
        *   The school maintains ongoing communication with the families to keep them informed of any developments. This includes updates on how the situation is being monitored and any further incidents or resolutions.
children.summary: The school communicates with the families of both the bully and victim via phone and written notice after an incident is reported and investigated, informing them of the situation, school actions, and available support. This process includes arranging meetings to discuss investigation findings, consequences for the bully, and protection measures for the victim, with ongoing updates provided.

children.title: Issuing the Warning and Consequences
children.content:
*   **Warning Issued**: The school issues the formal warning, which may include a written notice that outlines the incident, suspension, or exclusion following the bullying policy. The warning is documented in the student's record.
        *   **Expectations and Consequences**: The student is informed of the expectations for their future behaviour and the consequences they will face if they do not adhere to these expectations. This might include suspension or exclusion if the behaviour continues.
children.summary: This content describes the formal process for issuing a warning, which involves a written notice detailing the incident, potential suspension or exclusion according to policy, and documentation in the student's record. It also outlines how students are informed of future behavior expectations and the escalating consequences, such as further suspension or exclusion, for continued misbehavior.

children.title: Suspension Definition
children.content:
2.  **Suspension.**
    *   **Definition**: Suspension is a temporary removal of a student from their regular school environment due to misbehaviour or violation of school policies.
children.summary: The content defines "Suspension" as the temporary removal of a student from their regular school environment. This action is a consequence of misbehaviour or violation of school policies.

children.title: Actions and Consequences: Exclusion
children.content:
*   **Duration**: Suspensions can vary from a day to a few days or weeks, depending on the severity of the behaviour. The student is expected to return to school after the suspension period ends.
    *   **Purpose**: The main goal of suspension is to provide a consequence for the student's actions while also giving them time to reflect on their behaviour. It also serves as a warning to the student and others that certain behaviours are unacceptable.
    *   **Types**:
        *   In-School Suspension: The student remains at school but is isolated from their peers and regular activities.
        *   Out-of-School Suspension: The student is not allowed to attend school or participate in any school-related activities during the suspension period.

3.  **Exclusion.**
    *   **Definition**: Exclusion, also known as expulsion or permanent exclusion, is the permanent removal of a student from the school.
children.summary: This section outlines the duration, purpose, and types of suspensions, which can last from a day to weeks and include in-school or out-of-school isolation. It also defines exclusion as the permanent removal or expulsion of a student from school.

children.title: Reporting and Policy Review
children.content:
*   **Duration**: Exclusion is indefinite and means that the student is no longer allowed to attend the school from which they were excluded. The student would need to seek education elsewhere.
    *   **Purpose**: Exclusion is used as a last resort when a student's behaviour is so severe or repeated that the school believes they can no longer be a part of the school community. This might be due to actions that pose a significant risk to the safety or well-being of others or a repeated failure to adhere to school rules despite previous interventions.
    *   **Process**: Exclusion usually involves a formal process, including meetings with the student, parents or guardians, and school administrators. There may be an appeal process where the decision can be reviewed.

This may be reported to the local authority.

These are minimum sanctions. In very serious cases, it may be necessary to make a report to the Police or Social Services. However, it is the policy of the school to attempt to resolve such issues internally using our own disciplinary sanctions, unless the matter is of such gravity that a criminal prosecution is likely.

The staff will discuss this policy and its implications on their practice at least once every year. Where possible, parents will be invited to contribute to the school’s policy and procedures to prevent bullying. The records of bullying offences will be reviewed by the Management Team once a year at their ‘Dashboard Meeting’ at the end of each school year.

In parallel, we will tackle the issue within the year group/class.

Each incident will be discussed at the weekly staff meeting to inform the other members of staff about how it has been addressed and prevent further incidents.
children.summary: The school's reporting and policy review section defines exclusion as an indefinite, last-resort sanction for severe behavior, involving a formal process and potential reporting to the local authority. It further details annual policy reviews by staff and management, weekly incident discussions, and the school's preference for internal resolution over external reporting unless criminal prosecution is likely.

children.title: 6.12. Record of the Incident
children.content:
Details of the incident will be recorded by the welfare responsible (Lindsay &/or Sheila). The Guide will discuss with the named person for behaviour support what the appropriate and most helpful course of action is.

This may include:

*   A formal bullying warning where it is made clear what the bullying incident was and what would constitute a continuation of the bullying, including discussion of the incident or process in a hurtful way.
*   Clear strategies on what work would help both children (or groups of children) to be kind and loving towards each other.

**Monitoring and Review**

The Anti-Bullying Policy will be reviewed annually by the school’s Leadership Team.

Staff members will discuss the policy during designated professional development sessions to ensure consistency and effectiveness in its application. Records of bullying incidents will be analyzed for patterns, trends, and effectiveness of interventions.

**Reference Documents**

*   Imagine Montessori School Behaviour Policy
*   Imagine Montessori School Child Protection Policy
*   Local Authority Guidelines on Anti-Bullying Practices
*   United Nations Convention on the Rights of the Child (UNCRC)
*   National Anti-Bullying Alliance Resources
*   School’s PSHEE and Citizenship Curriculum Framework
children.summary: Welfare staff (Lindsay &/or Sheila) are responsible for recording incident details, with the Guide consulting a behaviour support person to decide on actions such as formal warnings or strategies for promoting kindness. The Anti-Bullying Policy is reviewed annually by the Leadership Team, discussed in staff professional development, and incident records are analyzed, with six specific reference documents also listed.

children.title: 7. Drug and Alcohol Policy
children.summary: The Drug and Alcohol Policy broadly applies to all individuals and locations, strictly prohibiting the possession, use, or sale of substances including alcohol, tobacco, vapes, and drugs, with exceptions for prescribed medicines. It defines key terms, outlines statutory duties referencing Spanish legislation (Ley 28/2005, 42/2010) and DfE guidance, details incident management protocols for pupils and intoxicated parents, and mandates staff training.

children.title: 7.1. Scope
children.content:
This policy applies to all pupils, staff, visitors, and third-party contractors associated with the school. It encompasses all activities conducted on school premises, during school-related events off-site, and while pupils are travelling to and from school. The policy extends to incidents involving legal and illegal substances, including alcohol, tobacco, vape pens, solvents, prescribed and over-the-counter drugs, and New Psychoactive Substances (NPS).

The aim of this Drug and Alcohol Policy is to acknowledge and clarify the school’s role in drug prevention and education, and ensure it is appropriate to meet pupils’ needs. This policy provides information about drug education, as well as procedures to respond to any drug-related incident.

This policy aims to ensure that the approach taken on the issue of drugs is a whole-school one and is part of our commitment to, and concern for, the overall health and well-being of the whole school community. Teachers will need to be confident and skilled to teach drug education, and pupils need to receive up-to-date, relevant, and accurate information, as well as support.

This policy should be read in conjunction with the policies and documents listed on the back page of this document.
children.summary: This policy outlines its broad applicability to all pupils, staff, visitors, and contractors, covering activities on school premises, off-site events, and travel to/from school. It specifically addresses incidents involving legal and illegal substances such as alcohol, tobacco, vape pens, solvents, prescribed/over-the-counter drugs, and New Psychoactive Substances (NPS).

children.title: 7.2. Terminology
children.content:
*   **Drugs**: Includes legal substances (alcohol, tobacco, vape pens, solvents), prescribed and over-the-counter medicines, and illegal substances (cannabis, ecstasy, amphetamines, heroin, cocaine, LSD, etc.).
*   **NPS**: New Psychoactive Substances, often referred to as "Legal Highs."
*   **Substance Misuse**: The use of any drug in a way that negatively impacts health, behaviour, or performance.
*   **Intoxicated**: Impaired by drugs or alcohol to a level that affects normal functioning.
children.summary: This section provides definitions for key terms related to substance use, including "Drugs" (categorized into legal, prescribed, and illegal types), "NPS" (New Psychoactive Substances), "Substance Misuse," and "Intoxicated." It clarifies the scope and meaning of these concepts.

children.title: 7.3. Statutory Duties
children.content:
Schools have a statutory duty to promote pupils’ wellbeing and hence have a clear role in preventing drug misuse as part of their pastoral care. Although there is no statutory requirement to have a Drug Policy, it is the advice of the DfE for schools to have a clear procedure for managing any incidents related to drugs/tobacco or alcohol misuse. It is essential to protect staff, parent/carer(s), children, and young people.

Spanish legislation (Ley 28/2005 de 26 de diciembre, as modified by Ley 42/2010 de 30 de diciembre) prohibits smoking in and around educational establishments. The Statutory Framework for the Early Years Foundation Stage (2021) requires the following (para 3.57): ‘Providers must not allow smoking, vaping, or the use of e-cigarettes in or on the premises when children are present or about to be present.’ This includes offsite trips and visits. Furthermore, practitioners must not be under the influence of alcohol or any other substances which may affect their ability to care for children, as per their Code of Conduct.
children.summary: Schools have a statutory duty to promote pupil wellbeing and prevent drug misuse, with the DfE advising clear procedures for managing related incidents. Specific prohibitions include Spanish legislation (Ley 28/2005, modified by Ley 42/2010) against smoking around educational establishments, the EYFS (2021, para 3.57) ban on smoking/vaping on premises when children are present, and a requirement for practitioners not to be under the influence of substances.

children.title: 7.4. Application
children.content:
*   This policy applies to all pupils on roll at the school. It applies on school property, when on educational visits, and when off-site at school-related events to ALL Imagine students, staff, parents, and third parties. This policy applies when pupils are travelling to and from school on public transport. Any responsibilities of ‘adults’ refers to employees and others acting in a supervisory role with pupils.
*   This policy also applies to all staff, visitors, and third-party contractors.
children.summary: This policy details its broad applicability to all pupils, staff, parents, visitors, and third-party contractors. It covers various locations including school property, educational visits, off-site school events, and travel to/from school on public transport, also defining 'adults' as employees or supervisors.

children.title: 7.5. The School’s Stance on Drugs, Health and the Needs of Pupils
children.content:
*   **Possession**: It is inappropriate and unacceptable for pupils to bring the above drugs into school, have them on their person, consume them before, during, and after school, and on the way to or from school, including on school visits, etc. IMS has a strict no smoking policy (including no vaping/e-cigarettes) on school sites. The drugs/substances covered by this policy are not to be purchased, sold, or otherwise exchanged during the school day or while pupils are on school visits.
*   **Use**: The school states that the use of drugs in school, during the school day, while travelling to/from school, or on school trips is inappropriate. Individual exceptions may be made for pupils who require prescription medicines, where appropriate and with the agreement of the Head of Primary or Secondary.
*   Legislation in Spain (*Ley 42/2010 de 30 de diciembre*) states that it is illegal to smoke on school premises. In Spain, it is also illegal to supply or sell alcoholic drinks in schools.
children.summary: The school strictly prohibits the possession, use, purchase, sale, or exchange of drugs, including smoking and vaping, on school premises, during school hours, or on school trips, with exceptions for prescribed medicines. This policy is reinforced by Spanish legislation (Ley 42/2010) which makes smoking and selling alcohol on school grounds illegal.

children.title: 7.6. Policy Framework
children.summary: The policy framework details the aims of drug education within the PSHE curriculum, outlining specific content delivered to students across Years 7-13, covering topics such as legal/illegal substances, definitions of dependence, and personal safety risks. It also establishes a protocol for inviting external visitors, like the Policia Nacional, to support these programs, requiring teacher presence and adherence to British, national, and local guidelines.

children.title: Drugs Education Aims
children.content:
**a. Drugs Education**

Drug education in our school aims to enable pupils to make healthy informed choices by increasing their knowledge, exploring a range of attitudes towards drug use, and developing and practising decision-making skills. The programme we follow will be based on British, national, and local guidelines for good practice and be appropriate to the age and experience of our pupils. Teaching will be based on an understanding that a variety of approaches should be used in order to meet the differing needs and learning styles of pupils.

We will provide all pupils with drug education as an integral part of our Personal, Social, Health and Economic (PSHE) programme, which includes:
children.summary: Drug education in the school aims to empower pupils to make healthy, informed choices by increasing their knowledge, exploring attitudes, and developing decision-making skills. This program is an integral part of the Personal, Social, Health and Economic (PSHE) curriculum and adheres to British, national, and local guidelines.

children.title: Content and Delivery: Years 7-9
children.content:
**b. Content and Delivery**

**Year 7 - 9**

*   The positive and negative roles played by drugs in society (including alcohol).
*   Information about legal and illegal substances, including alcohol, volatile substances, tobacco, and cannabis, and the law relating to their supply, use, and misuse.
*   To recognise and manage different influences on their decisions about the use of substances (including clarifying and challenging their own perceptions, values, and beliefs), including managing peer influence.
*   The personal and social risks and consequences of substance use and misuse, including the benefits of not drinking alcohol (or delaying the age at which to start) and the benefits of not smoking, including not harming others with second-hand smoke.
*   The safe use of prescribed and over-the-counter medicines.
*   The risks and consequences of ‘experimental’ and ‘occasional’ substance use and the terms ‘dependence’ and ‘addiction’.
*   About how to access local health services.
*   About cancer and cancer prevention, including healthy lifestyles, acknowledging that childhood cancers are rarely caused by lifestyle choices.
children.summary: The curriculum for Years 7-9 details the positive and negative societal roles of drugs, including alcohol, and information on legal and illegal substances like tobacco and cannabis, along with related laws. It also covers managing influences on substance decisions, personal risks, safe medicine use, definitions of dependence, accessing health services, and cancer prevention.

children.title: Content and Delivery: Years 10-11
children.content:
**Year 10 & 11 - 13**

*   The short and long-term consequences of substance use and misuse for the health and mental and emotional well-being of individuals, families, and communities, including the health risks related to second-hand smoke.
*   Understand the terms ‘habit’, ‘dependence’, and ‘addiction’ in relation to substance use and to whom to talk if they have concerns.
*   The wider risks and consequences of legal and illegal substance use, including on their personal safety, career, relationships, and future lifestyle.
children.summary: For students in Years 10-13, this content outlines the short and long-term consequences of substance use and misuse on health, mental well-being, families, and communities, including risks from second-hand smoke. It also defines 'habit', 'dependence', and 'addiction', and covers wider risks to personal safety, career, and relationships, along with identifying who to talk to for concerns.

children.title: Content and Delivery: Years 12-13
children.content:
**Year 12 & 13**

*   How to manage alcohol and drug use in relation to immediate and long-term health.
*   Understand alcohol and drug use can affect decision-making and personal safety.
*   Evaluate the impact of drugs and alcohol and their use on road safety, workplace safety, legal safety, reputation, and career.
*   Manage personal safety off-line, including when socialising (including meeting someone in person for the first time whom they met online, drink spiking, looking out for friends) and travelling (especially cycle safety, young driver safety, and passenger safety, including the risks of being a passenger with an intoxicated driver, and using only licensed taxis).
children.summary: For Years 12-13, the content details how to manage alcohol and drug use, evaluating its impact on immediate and long-term health, decision-making, and safety across road, workplace, legal, reputation, and career domains. It also covers offline personal safety, addressing socialising risks like meeting online contacts, drink spiking, and looking out for friends, alongside travel safety specifics such as cycle, young driver, and passenger safety, including risks with intoxicated drivers and using licensed taxis.

children.title: Use of Visitors and Outside Speakers
children.content:
**c. Use of Visitors and Outside Speakers**

We invite visitors such as the Policia Nacional to support our planned teacher-led programme of education, in line with British, national, and local guidance. We are careful to negotiate the use of visitors in line with the protocol for using visitors in IMS, so that their contribution fits our needs and they are clear how their input fits into our planned programme. Teachers are always present when visitors are working with our pupils.
children.summary: The school invites visitors, like the Policia Nacional, to support teacher-led educational programs, following British, national, and local guidance. A specific protocol is used to negotiate visitor contributions, ensuring they fit the school's needs and planned programs, with teachers always present during their interactions with pupils.

children.title: 7.7. Staff Support and Training
children.content: The school is committed to providing drug awareness training in the induction of all staff working at the school and on an ongoing basis for staff involved in drugs education.
children.summary: The school commits to providing drug awareness training for all staff during their induction. Ongoing training is also provided for staff specifically involved in drugs education.

children.title: 7.8. Management of an Incident
children.content:
*   Where there is evidence or suspicion that a pupil has drugs in their possession, has used drugs, or has passed on/sold drugs to another person, the school will initially seek medical attention for the child if needed, as per 7.3.
*   The school will follow its Behaviour and Exclusion Policies and may also use its power to search a pupil. Sanctions will be applied as outlined in the Behaviour and Exclusion Policies.
*   Where it is believed that a parent or carer is under the influence of drugs (including alcohol) on the school premises, they will be asked to leave. If they are due to collect their child, then they will be asked to make alternative arrangements, and the child will be withheld to ensure their safety. If a parent or carer refuses to leave, the Headteacher or their representative should call the police. Where there are serious or ongoing concerns about the adult’s presentation and drug use, the school may consider banning a parent from entering the school site.
*   See Appendix B – Responding to incidents involving drugs.
children.summary: The school's protocol for pupil drug incidents prioritizes medical attention, followed by applying behaviour policies, potential searches, and sanctions. For parents/carers under the influence on school premises, the policy outlines asking them to leave, arranging alternative child collection, calling the police if they refuse, and potentially banning them from the site.

children.title: 7.9. Police and Emergency Involvement
children.content:
*   While there is no legal obligation to inform the police, they may be involved at the discretion of the Headteacher in consultation with the Director. The school will consider each incident individually and will employ a range of responses to deal with individual incidents. The Headteachers will inform the Director in relation to all drug-related incidents.
*   See Appendix A for guidance on drug situations linked to medical emergencies.
children.summary: Police involvement in incidents is at the Headteacher's discretion, in consultation with the Director, despite no legal obligation. The Headteacher must inform the Director of all drug-related incidents, and Appendix A offers guidance for drug situations involving medical emergencies.

children.title: 7.10. The Needs of Pupils
children.content: The school will exercise its pastoral responsibilities and always look to support pupils and ensure that pupils have access to appropriate support and guidance.
children.summary: The school commits to exercising its pastoral responsibilities. It will always look to support pupils and ensure they have access to appropriate guidance.

children.title: 7.11. Information Sharing
children.content: If the school has concerns about a child’s safety, the school will share information with law enforcement agencies and safeguarding agencies as outlined in the Safeguarding Policy. This includes working in partnership with external agencies.
children.summary: If there are concerns about a child's safety, the school will share information with law enforcement and safeguarding agencies, including other external agencies. This process is outlined in the Safeguarding Policy.

children.title: 7.12. Involvement of Parent/Carer(s)
children.content: The school will inform parents and carers of any drug-related incidents involving their own child, unless in very rare circumstances this would heighten the risk to the child.
children.summary: The school commits to informing parents and carers about any drug-related incidents involving their child. This policy includes a specific exception for very rare circumstances where such notification would heighten the child's risk.

children.title: 7.13. Staff Conduct and Drug Use
children.content: All staff are required to adhere to the Staff Code of Conduct. Staff are subject to the IMS disciplinary policy and other HR procedures should they breach policy.
children.summary: Staff are required to adhere to the Staff Code of Conduct. Breaches of this policy will result in disciplinary action under the IMS disciplinary policy and other HR procedures.

children.title: 7.14. The Role of the Headteacher
children.content:
*   The Secondary Headteacher takes overall responsibility for the implementation of this policy.
*   The Secondary Headteacher will ensure that all staff dealing with substance issues are adequately trained and supported.
*   IMS cannot knowingly allow premises to be used for the production or supply of any controlled drug. Where it is suspected that substances are sold on the premises, details of those involved, together with as much information as possible, will be automatically passed to the police.
children.summary: The Secondary Headteacher is responsible for the overall implementation of this policy and for ensuring staff training and support regarding substance issues. The school prohibits the use of its premises for drug production or supply, and any suspected drug sales will lead to automatic notification of the police with all available details.

children.title: 7.15. Specific Procedures
children.content:
**Dealing with Information and Confidentiality**

*   The school Safeguarding and Child Protection Policy and Procedures must be followed. Staff cannot and should not promise confidentiality to the pupil.
*   The following questions should also be considered:
    *   How serious is the situation?
    *   What immediate and significant risk does the young person face?
    *   What implications (both positive and negative) could keeping a confidence have?
    *   Could there be a transfer of risk to other children?
    *   Could significant harm result from keeping the young person’s disclosure confidential?

**Parental Substance Misuse**

*   Parental substance misuse has the potential to impact negatively on outcomes for children and young people.
*   Substance use is not a reason for considering a child to be suffering or at risk of suffering significant harm, although it may be a contributing factor.
*   The Child Protection and Wellbeing Coordinator or Head will liaise with the police and/or external agencies where required.

**Controlled Drugs (including New Psychoactive Substances)**

In taking temporary possession of suspected controlled drugs, the school will:

*   Ensure that a second adult witness is present throughout.

**Searching, Screening, and Confiscations**

Headteachers and staff authorised by them have a statutory power to search pupils or their possessions without consent where they have reasonable grounds for suspecting that the pupil may have a prohibited item. Searches must be done with 2 members of staff present.
children.summary: The content details specific procedures for handling information and confidentiality, emphasizing that staff cannot promise it, and addresses parental substance misuse, noting it's not a sole reason for significant harm. It also outlines that two adult witnesses are required for temporary possession of controlled drugs, and two staff members must be present for searches of pupils or their possessions for prohibited items, which can be conducted without consent.

children.title: 7.16. Monitoring and Review
children.content:
The Leadership Team will review the policy annually or following significant incidents or legislative changes.

Updates will be communicated to all stakeholders, ensuring the policy remains relevant and effective.
children.summary: The Leadership Team is responsible for reviewing the policy annually or following significant incidents or legislative changes. Updates resulting from these reviews will be communicated to all stakeholders to ensure the policy's continued relevance and effectiveness.

children.title: 7.17. Reference Documents
children.content:
*   Child Protection Policy
*   Behaviour Policy
*   Code of Conduct Document
*   Statutory Framework for the Early Years Foundation Stage (2021)
*   Ley 28/2005 de 26 de diciembre
*   Ley 42/2010 de 30 de diciembre
*   Department for Education Guidance on Managing Drug-Related Incidents
children.summary: This section enumerates critical reference documents, including foundational policies on Child Protection and Behaviour, a Code of Conduct, and the Statutory Framework for the Early Years Foundation Stage (2021). It also lists specific legal acts (Ley 28/2005, Ley 42/2010) and Department for Education Guidance on Managing Drug-Related Incidents, providing essential rules and guidelines for operations.

children.title: 8. Weapons and Dangerous Items Policy
children.summary: This policy establishes a zero-tolerance approach to weapons and dangerous items across all school premises and events, defining key terms like "weapon" and "prohibited item." It details staff's authority to search and confiscate, outlines immediate actions for incidents including reporting to authorities, and mandates annual review and the use of safety plans for pupils.

children.title: 8.1. Scope
children.content: This policy applies to all students, staff, parents, visitors, and any other individuals accessing the Imagine Montessori School (IMS) site. It outlines the preventative measures, actions, and procedures in place to address the presence or use of weapons within the school grounds, as well as the support and safety measures provided to our community in such situations. This policy covers all areas of the school premises, during school hours, and at any school-organized events or activities, whether on or off-site.
children.summary: This policy specifies the individuals (students, staff, parents, visitors) and locations (all school premises, during school hours, and at all school-organized events, on or off-site) to which it applies. It outlines the preventative measures, actions, and procedures for addressing weapons, as well as the support and safety measures provided.

children.title: 8.2. Terminology
children.content:
*   **Weapon**: For the purposes of this policy, a weapon includes but is not limited to:
    *   Firearms, including starting pistols, air guns, pellet guns, BB guns, and replicas or toy guns.
    *   Bladed objects such as knives, craft knives, scissors, and razor blades.
    *   Explosives, including fireworks.
    *   Other objects like aerosol sprays, lighters, matches, laser pens, and martial arts equipment (e.g., nunchaku, death stars).
    *   Tools such as screwdrivers, hammers, and chisels that can be offensively used.
*   **Prohibited Item**: Any object identified under this policy that poses a potential risk to the safety and well-being of the school community.
*   **Safety Plan**: A structured intervention created for a pupil following a weapon-related incident, aimed at ensuring their safety and the safety of others while promoting positive behaviour and support.
children.summary: This section defines key terms relevant to the policy, specifically "Weapon," "Prohibited Item," and "Safety Plan." It provides detailed examples for various types of weapons and clarifies the scope and purpose of prohibited items and safety plans.

children.title: 8.3. Policy Statement and Principles
children.content:
1.  **Commitment to Safety**: At IMS, the safety and well-being of all community members is a top priority. Immediate action will always be taken to reduce risks and ensure a secure environment for everyone on-site.
2.  **Preventative Culture**:
    *   IMS promotes a strong safeguarding culture where staff, students, and parents are vigilant and proactive in addressing potential risks.
    *   The Personal, Social, Health, and Economic (PSHE) curriculum educates students on the dangers and legal implications of carrying or using weapons.
    *   Open communication between the school and parents is essential to reinforce a shared responsibility in addressing safety concerns.
3.  **Responsiveness and Individual Context**:
    *   Every incident involving a weapon will be handled based on the individual circumstances, intent, and level of risk posed.
    *   Decisions will prioritize the safety of all while considering the needs of the pupil involved, with actions ranging from exclusion to safety planning and support.
4.  **Zero Tolerance**:
    *   Weapons of any kind are strictly prohibited on school premises.
    *   Staff will take immediate action upon suspicion or discovery of a weapon, including searches, confiscation, and necessary reporting to authorities.
children.summary: The policy outlines IMS's commitment to safety, a preventative culture, and a zero-tolerance stance on weapons, which are strictly prohibited on school premises. It details that staff will take immediate action, including searches and reporting to authorities, and that incidents will be handled individually, prioritizing safety while considering the pupil's needs.

children.title: 8.4. Purpose and Aim
children.summary: This section details the policy's purpose to prevent weapon use and outlines IMS's responses to incidents, providing a comprehensive definition of a "weapon" that includes firearms, bladed objects, replicas, and tools like screwdrivers. It also mandates staff vigilance, uses the curriculum to educate pupils on weapon risks, and defines parental responsibilities regarding the policy.

children.title: Purpose and Aim of the Policy
children.content:
At Imagine Montessori School, the purpose of this policy guidance is to clearly set out the preventative actions in place to prevent the use of any weapons on our site. This policy guidance will also show the direct actions a school will take around the use of weapons on site, taking into account the full context around each and every incident.

At IMS, we are committed to the safety and well-being of all of our community and will always take immediate action to reduce the risk of harm to any individual on our school site.

**
children.summary: This section details the policy's purpose: to outline preventative actions against weapon use and the direct responses Imagine Montessori School (IMS) will take in incidents, considering the full context. It also highlights IMS's commitment to community safety and well-being, ensuring immediate action to reduce harm.

children.title: Definition of a Weapon
children.content:
What is a weapon?**

For the purpose of this Policy, a “weapon” is: a firearm of any description, including starting pistols, air guns, and any type of replica or toy gun, pellet guns, or BB guns; knives, including all variations of bladed objects, i.e., pocket knives, craft knives, scissors, etc.; explosives, including fireworks, aerosol sprays, lighters, matches; laser pens or other objects, even if manufactured for a non-violent purpose, but have a potentially violent use, i.e., the purpose of keeping or carrying the object is for use, or threat of use, as a weapon. Nunchucks, death stars, and other martial arts objects. Screwdrivers, hammers, chisels, and any tool that could be offensively used. Razors, razor blades, or chains.

**
children.summary: This content provides a comprehensive definition of a "weapon" for policy purposes, listing specific categories such as firearms, bladed objects, explosives, and martial arts tools.
